claude-sonnet-4-6

+ Mythology-Inspired — Gods Wore Sneakers (Chapter Avg: 80.8 | Final: 75.1)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Mythology-Inspired Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: Gods wore sneakers.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Mythology-Inspired Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: Gods wore sneakers. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming ## The Core Premise "Gods wore sneakers" — this is a delightful collision of the divine and the mundane. The sneaker is a distinctly modern, democratic, casual object. Gods are ancient, hierarchical, formal. The tension is the story. ## Possible Interpretations **Literal:** Actual mythological gods (Greek, Norse, Hindu, West African, etc.) are living on Earth in the modern day and literally wearing sneakers. The sneakers matter somehow — they ground them, limit them, signify something. **Metaphorical:** "Gods" refers to people worshipped as gods — celebrities, influencers, athletes — and sneakers are the symbol of that worship (sneaker culture, limited drops, etc.). A story about modern idolatry. **Hybrid:** Real gods have descended/integrated into modern society, and sneaker culture becomes the lens through which their divinity is explored or concealed or diminished. **Mythological creation story:** A story told in the style of mythology that incorporates sneakers as a sacred/foundational object — a kind of invented myth. **Reversal:** Humans ascend to godhood, and sneakers are the last human thing they hold onto. ## Possible Characters - **Hermes/Mercury** — most obvious fit. God of travelers, messengers, speed. Winged sandals → sneakers is a natural evolution. A trickster figure navigating modern life. - **Ares** — god of war wearing combat boots... but what if he's gone soft, wearing Air Jordans, coaching youth basketball? - **Demeter** — goddess of harvest, now a farmer's market vendor, sensible New Balance. - **Persephone** — trapped between worlds, wearing Converse, working in a florist shop that also sells things from the underworld. - **Hades** — an understated, elegant man in all-black who wears very clean, very white sneakers — the irony of it. - **A mortal protagonist** — someone who stumbles into contact with the gods, or who works alongside them unknowingly. A sneaker store employee? A sneaker designer? - **Norse gods:** Loki as a sneaker reseller/scammer. Thor as a gym bro in lifting shoes. Odin as a homeless-looking man in beat-up Nikes who sees everything. - **Anansi** (West African/Caribbean) — a trickster god who runs a pawn shop or a shoe repair shop. - **A lesser god/demigod** — a more relatable figure, semi-divine, trying to fit in. ## Possible Plots **Plot A — The Grounding Plot:** Sneakers are literally what tether gods to the mortal realm. As long as they wear them, they're bound to Earth. One god is trying to take theirs off and return home. Stakes: what happens when a god leaves? **Plot B — The New Pantheon:** A young mortal woman working at a sneaker shop slowly realizes her regulars are gods. Each chapter introduces a new god/customer. She becomes important to them somehow — she's the last true believer? She's unknowingly divine herself? **Plot C — The Fallen God:** A single god, stripped of powers, forced to live as a mortal. The sneakers are a mark of their humiliation or their new identity. A journey to reclaim divinity or choose humanity. **Plot D — The Contest:** Gods are competing in a modern context (a race, a sneaker design competition, something urban and contemporary) and the story follows the contest and its consequences. **Plot E — The Shoe as Relic:** A pair of ancient sneakers (impossibly old, yet pristine) appears and whoever wears them is touched by divinity. A grounded magical realism approach. **Plot F — Hermes' POV:** Hermes, the most adaptable of gods, narrates his existence in the modern world — he's a delivery driver or a courier. The sneakers are his new winged sandals. The story is about a delivery he must make that has cosmic consequences. ## Tone Options - Warm, humorous, Terry Pratchett-esque - Literary, melancholy, mythic (like Madeline Miller) - Urban fantasy, gritty and streetwise - Magical realism, quiet and beautiful (like Gabriel García Márquez) - YA-adjacent, coming-of-age ## Story Beat Ideas - A god being clocked/recognized by a mortal - The moment sneakers are taken off — something shifts - A god doing something embarrassingly human - Two gods meeting and the tension of their divine relationship playing out in mundane context - A mortal choosing to help or hinder a god - A sacrifice or bargain - A revelation of true nature/form ## Setting Options - A city (New York, Chicago, Lagos, Athens modern-day) - A small town where gods are hidden in plain sight - A sneaker store as hub/anchor point --- # Reflection The metaphorical reading (celebrities as gods) is clever but feels like it moves away from the mythology-inspired requirement of the prompt. It's a good essay idea but probably not a rich enough mythological story. Setting it aside. The "invented myth" approach — writing as if sneakers ARE ancient sacred objects — is charming and fun but risks being a one-note joke stretched too thin over 8,000 words. **What seems to work best** is the hybrid literal/modern approach: actual mythological gods living in the contemporary world, and sneakers matter narratively and thematically, not just as a visual detail. **The Hermes POV (Plot F)** is very compelling. Hermes is the most naturally "streetwise" and adaptable of the gods, the trickster-messenger. Making him a courier/delivery driver with high-end sneakers replacing his winged sandals is elegant. His job would bring him into contact with other gods and mortals. The sneakers could have actual power — speed, direction-finding — vestigial divine properties in a new form. This gives the story a clear protagonist with a defined voice. **The sneaker store as hub (Plot B)** is also strong, giving a mortal anchor character. But 8 chapters may be better served with a divine protagonist whose interiority we can access — mortals discovering gods can feel repetitive chapter to chapter. **What I'd combine:** Hermes as narrator/protagonist, delivery driver in a modern city, wearing a beat-up but impossibly fast pair of sneakers. The story follows one significant delivery — a package of cosmic importance — that brings him into contact with several other gods living in the city. Each chapter moves the delivery forward while revealing the state of the gods in the modern world. The climax: what's in the package, and what choice must Hermes make? **Tone:** Terry Pratchett meets Madeline Miller — warm humor on the surface, genuine mythic weight underneath. First person or close third, Hermes' voice being wry and world-weary but not cynical. **The sneakers as theme:** They represent adaptation, humility, groundedness. The gods who wear them well are the ones who've accepted the modern world. The gods who resent them are dangerous. Hermes loves his sneakers — that tells us everything about him. **Pacing over 8 chapters:** - Ch 1: Establish Hermes, the city, the sneakers, the delivery job, the mysterious package - Ch 2-3: Early stops on the delivery route — meet 1-2 gods living diminished/changed lives - Ch 4: Midpoint complication — the package is more dangerous than thought, someone wants to intercept it - Ch 5-6: Higher stakes stops — more powerful/dangerous gods, the world tilting - Ch 7: Crisis — Hermes must choose between divine duty and something he's come to care about - Ch 8: Resolution — the delivery is made (or not), and what it means for gods in the modern world This structure works. It's a road/journey story (appropriate for Hermes), episodic enough to feel like connected myths, but with a through-line that builds. 8 chapters × ~1000 words = room to breathe but not bloat. One concern: introducing too many gods could feel like a checklist. Solution — limit it to 4-5 gods encountered, making each encounter genuinely different in tone and stakes, not just "here's another god being quirky." The ending should have genuine mythic weight — not just a joke landing. Perhaps what Hermes delivers is something that could restore the gods' full power, or something that would end them. His choice to deliver it or not is the story's soul.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention ## Core Concept This story follows **Hermes** — god of messengers, travelers, and thieves — now working as a courier in a modern American city (Chicago, for its grid-like streets that suit a delivery driver, and its mythic nickname "the Windy City" which carries divine resonance). He narrates in close third person, past tense, with a voice that is wry, observational, and laced with the melancholy of someone who has seen everything and is still, somehow, surprised by humans. The tone sits between Terry Pratchett's warmth and Madeline Miller's mythic gravity — jokes that have weight, beauty that has a bite. ## The Sneakers Hermes wears a pair of battered Nike Air Max — grey, with a barely-visible iridescent sheen that most people write off as a trick of the light. They are his winged sandals, transformed. They don't fly, exactly, but he never misses a green light. He always finds parking. Crowds part without knowing why. The sneakers are the story's central symbol: **adaptation as survival**. The gods who have made peace with the modern world wear sneakers. The gods who haven't are in trouble — frozen in grandeur, worshipped by no one, quietly going mad or going dark. The sneakers are humility made material. Hermes loves his. That matters. ## The Plot Hermes works for a courier company called **WING** (he finds this funny; no one else knows why). On an ordinary Tuesday, he is assigned a delivery that is flagged *Priority Absolute* — a category that, in the company's digital system, shouldn't exist. The package is small, dense, warm to the touch, and addressed to someone at a location that Hermes recognises with a cold dread he hasn't felt in centuries. The delivery will take him across Chicago, bringing him into contact with **four other gods** living diminished, disguised, or dangerously unmoored lives. Each encounter reveals something about the package, about the state of divinity in the modern world, and about what Hermes himself has been avoiding. The package contains — as he eventually understands — the last concentrated **ember of genuine belief**: the final residue of real, mythic faith that still exists in the world, siphoned and condensed into physical form by means he doesn't fully understand. Whoever receives it could use it to reignite something. Or to end the gods entirely — a clean, painless extinction, the last light going out. The address belongs to **Zeus**, who Hermes hasn't seen in decades, who has been waiting. ## Characters - **Hermes** — protagonist, courier, wry and fast and more emotionally guarded than he lets on. His arc is about choosing to care, after millennia of detachment. - **Demeter** — runs a struggling farmer's market stall. Grief-hollowed, still beautiful, growing things in a city that doesn't notice. Gives Hermes the first real warning about the package. - **Ares** — owns a gym on the South Side. Angry, diminished, but not villainous — more like a man who has forgotten what he was for. Wants the package for uncomplicated, dangerous reasons. - **Persephone** — works at a florist/funeral home on the North Side. The most adapted of all the gods Hermes meets, the most at peace. She is the story's moral compass, and her acceptance of the human world is what shifts something in Hermes. - **Zeus** — the recipient. Seen only in the final chapter. Not monstrous, not magnificent. Something more devastating: **tired**. His intentions with the package are the story's final revelation. ## What I'm Aiming For - Each god encounter should feel **tonally distinct** — Demeter's chapter is autumnal and sad, Ares' is tense and kinetic, Persephone's is quietly luminous. - The sneakers should appear **meaningfully** in each chapter — different gods, different footwear choices, each one telling. - The mythological references should feel **earned and unforced** — readers familiar with the myths will find resonance, readers unfamiliar will not feel lost. - The ending should be **ambiguous but not unsatisfying** — Hermes makes a choice, and the story trusts the reader to understand its weight. - Avoid: a comedic tone that deflates genuine stakes; an ending that resolves too cleanly; gods who feel like costumes rather than characters; sneakers as mere background detail rather than live symbol. --- # Chapter Planning ## Chapter 1 — *Wing Tips* (~1000 words) **Purpose:** Establish Hermes, the world, the tone, and the inciting object. Hermes begins his Tuesday shift at WING Courier's dispatch hub. We meet him through his routine: the city at dawn, the pleasure he takes in movement, his sneakers. He reflects briefly on what he is and what he has become — not with self-pity, but with the pragmatic acceptance of someone who has survived by adapting. He picks up his delivery manifest and finds the anomalous package waiting for him, already assigned and non-reassignable. It is warm. The label gives a pickup address he recognises and a delivery address that stops him cold. He knows the name. He hasn't said it in a long time. He loads his bag, laces his sneakers, and steps into the city. **Sneaker beat:** He ties his laces with a ritualistic precision — three times around, a specific knot. An old habit. The sneakers hum faintly when he picks up the package. --- ## Chapter 2 — *Green Thumb* (~1000 words) **Purpose:** First god encounter; establish the state of the divine in the modern world; first warning. His route takes him to the Green City Market near Lincoln Park, where Demeter runs a stall — organic produce, heritage grains, honey from bees that always find flowers even in winter. She is weathered in the way old photographs are weathered — beautiful detail becoming harder to see. She isn't surprised to see him. She offers him coffee, and they speak in the elliptical way old beings speak to each other, circling what they mean. She notices the package and goes still. She tells him to be careful who he tells about it. She doesn't say why. Before he leaves, she presses a pomegranate into his hands — then seems embarrassed by the instinct, like a reflex she can't help. **Sneaker beat:** Demeter wears no sneakers. She's barefoot despite the cold, feet dirty with soil. Her feet touch the ground and the ground responds. She eyes Hermes' Nikes and says, *"You always did know how to travel light."* --- ## Chapter 3 — *Iron* (~1000 words) **Purpose:** Second god encounter; raise stakes; introduce threat to the package. The route takes Hermes to the South Side, delivering a separate package to a boxing gym above a laundromat — IRON & GLORY, owned by Ares. The gym smells like effort and iron and old wood. Ares is bigger than he needs to be, coaching a teenager on footwork, and he is good at it despite everything. The interaction is careful, two people who have a complicated history. Ares clocks the special package in Hermes' bag — he can feel it the way all old things can feel it — and wants to know where it's going. Hermes deflects. Ares lets him leave but the warning is clear: *someone else already came through here asking questions.* The package is known. The delivery just got harder. **Sneaker beat:** Ares wears battered high-top Jordans, red and black, worn until the sole is separating. He hasn't bought new ones. He doesn't see the point. --- ## Chapter 4 — *The Long Way* (~1000 words) **Purpose:** Midpoint; Hermes alone with the package; deepening internal conflict. Between stops, Hermes sits in his parked van eating a gyro and thinking. This chapter slows down — it's interior, reflective, a pause before the storm. He considers the package: what he knows, what he suspects, what it means that Zeus is at the end of this route. He thinks about the nature of belief — how it felt to be believed in completely, how that feeling has thinned over centuries to almost nothing. He takes the package out and holds it. It is warm. He thinks he can feel something like a heartbeat inside it. He doesn't open it. He thinks about the last time he saw Zeus, and what was said. He puts the package back, starts the van, and keeps driving. But we understand now that he is afraid — not of Ares, not of interception, but of arriving. **Sneaker beat:** He catches his reflection in the van's side mirror — just his feet and ankles visible, the sneakers on the Chicago asphalt. He thinks: *every road-god needs good shoes*. It steadies him. --- ## Chapter 5 — *Perennial* (~1000 words) **Purpose:** Third god encounter; the emotional pivot of the story; Persephone as moral compass. Hermes stops at a flower and funeral shop on the North Side — *Perennial* — run by Persephone. She is the most surprising god he's encountered today: genuinely happy. Or not happy exactly, but **settled**. She arranges flowers for a funeral and talks to him while she works, asking real questions about him — not the package, not Zeus, not the old world. Him. What does he like about the city? What's his favourite route? She is the only one who treats him as a person rather than a divine instrument. This rattles Hermes more than Ares did. She knows about the package — of course she does, she's been moving between worlds longer than anyone — and she tells him what she believes it really is. She also tells him that the choice of what to do with it belongs to the one who carries it, not the one who receives it. She says this lightly, arranging white chrysanthemums. It lands like a stone. **Sneaker beat:** Persephone wears simple white canvas sneakers — Converse, old, clean, with small flowers drawn in ballpoint pen around the rubber sole. She drew them herself. It's the most human thing in the story so far. --- ## Chapter 6 — *Interception* (~1000 words) **Purpose:** Action and tension; the threat becomes real; Hermes' skill and identity on display. Someone tries to take the package. Not Ares himself — someone working for him, or working for someone else, or perhaps just desperate: a figure who is almost divine, a minor deity or a very old demigod, running on fumes of fading belief. The attempt happens on the street, in traffic, in the particular chaos of a Chicago afternoon. This chapter is kinetic — Hermes in his element, moving through the city faster than he should be able to, the sneakers doing their quiet impossible work. He doesn't fight so much as he *flows* — misdirects, outpaces, disappears into the grid of streets. The thief is not caught but is lost. Afterwards, Hermes is exhilarated and then immediately, honestly scared. The package is real. The stakes are real. He has to decide what he's actually going to do. **Sneaker beat:** During the chase, the iridescent sheen on his Nikes becomes unmistakable — a brief flash of actual gold light on the sole, gone before anyone can be sure they saw it. --- ## Chapter 7 — *The Address* (~1000 words) **Purpose:** Arrival; the penultimate confrontation; Hermes makes his choice. Hermes arrives at the address — a modest, unremarkable house in a quiet neighbourhood, the kind of house that is difficult to remember clearly once you've looked away. Zeus answers the door. The chapter earns its weight here. Zeus is old — not physically (he looks sixty, fit, careful) but in every other way: old in the eyes, old in the way he stands, old in the way he thanks Hermes for coming like a man who didn't expect anyone to show up. They sit. Zeus explains what he wants the package for: not power, not restoration. He wants to use the last ember of belief to **release them** — to burn through the last tether keeping the gods bound to a world that no longer needs them. A clean ending. A chosen one. He believes this is mercy. Hermes holds the package. He asks: *what if I don't deliver it?* **Sneaker beat:** Zeus is in bare feet on the hardwood floor. He notices Hermes' sneakers and says nothing for a long moment. Then: *"You always did love roads."* It is not an insult. It might be the kindest thing he's ever said to him. --- ## Chapter 8 — *Delivery* (~1000 words) **Purpose:** Resolution; Hermes' choice and its meaning; the ending. Hermes doesn't give Zeus the package. He doesn't keep it either. He takes it back into the city — to the market, to the gym, to the flower shop, and to three or four other places, ordinary places, where he breaks the package open and lets what's inside dissipate into the air like heat. Whatever belief remains, he returns it to the city. It's not enough to restore anything. It won't make anyone pray again. But it disperses like seeds, like pollen, like the smell before rain — into a schoolyard, a hospital, a baseball diamond, a laundromat. Small warmths. The gods will go on as they are: diminished, adapted, wearing sneakers. This is neither tragedy nor triumph. This is what endurance looks like. The final image: Hermes back in his van, a new manifest loading on his phone. Another delivery. He puts the van in drive. His sneakers rest on the pedals. He goes. **Sneaker beat:** When he breaks the package open, just for a moment, the sneakers are fully golden — actual light, actual wings, actual speed — and then it fades, and they are just shoes again, grey and worn and his. He looks down at them. He smiles.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# How A Human Writer Might Approach This ## The Human Starting Point A human writer coming to "Gods wore sneakers" would likely start from a **feeling** rather than a structure. They might be drawn to the prompt because it reminds them of something — a specific person they know who seems larger than life, the uncanny experience of seeing something ancient in something modern, their own complicated relationship with belief or with feeling overlooked by the universe. The story would have a **personal gravitational centre** that isn't visible on the page but shapes every decision. A human writer might also resist the most obvious interpretation for longer — might write three failed drafts going in weird directions before landing on something. That resistance and failure leaves traces in the final work. It creates the slightly unexpected angle, the detail that doesn't quite fit the thesis but feels true, the moment where the story surprises even its author. A human writer working on this specific prompt might be thinking about: - A real person they know who has "fallen" from some great height and is now living a smaller life - Their own feelings about faith — lost, found, complicated - The genuine philosophical question of what gods would *feel* in modernity — not as a clever premise but as something that genuinely unsettles them - Sneaker culture specifically — drop days, the resale market, the way people queue overnight for shoes — and what that says about modern worship - The comedy of the divine made mundane, yes, but also the tragedy of it, which they'd hold simultaneously without resolving the tension ## Telltale LLM Approaches to Writing (Generally) ### 1. Completionism LLMs tend to want to **cover everything**. Every character gets an arc. Every theme gets addressed. Every setup gets a payoff. Human writers leave things hanging — not from sloppiness but because life leaves things hanging, and they trust readers. An LLM will often produce a story that is, structurally, too complete. It answers questions the reader was enjoying sitting with. ### 2. Safe Emotional Beats LLMs trend toward emotions that are **legible and socially acceptable**. Characters feel sad about sad things, hopeful about hopeful things. The more complex, socially awkward, or morally uncomfortable emotions — pettiness, schadenfreude, the specific shame of being witnessed in a small moment, the way grief can feel like boredom — tend to get flattened out. LLMs write *about* complexity rather than *from* it. ### 3. Impressive Writing Over True Writing There's a tendency to reach for the **literary-sounding phrase** — the metaphor that announces itself, the sentence that wants to be quoted. This comes at the cost of the plain sentence that hits harder precisely because it isn't trying. Human writers learn (often painfully) that the most powerful moments are often the most unadorned ones. LLMs tend to over-write emotional peaks. ### 4. Adjective and Adverb Overreach Related to the above: LLMs **over-describe**. Every noun wants an adjective, every verb wants an adverb. Human writers, especially experienced ones, learn to trust nouns and verbs to do the work. The difference between "he walked slowly and sadly down the quiet, empty street" and "he walked home" — the latter, in context, being more devastating. ### 5. Symmetry and Tidiness LLMs love **symbolic closure** — the thing introduced in chapter one returns in chapter eight, transformed. This can be beautiful when organic, but LLMs do it mechanically, which makes it feel like a thesis being proven rather than a life being lived. Symbols that resolve too cleanly stop being symbols and become diagrams. ### 6. Character Voice Homogeneity Even when LLMs plan distinct characters, the **actual prose voice** often homogenizes. Characters end up sounding like variations of the narrator rather than genuinely different people with different verbal textures, rhythms, and blind spots. ### 7. The Neat Theme Statement LLMs have a tendency to have a character — often near the climax — **say the theme out loud**. "This is what endurance looks like." "The choice belongs to the one who carries it, not the one who receives it." These are thesis statements dressed as dialogue. Human writers usually bury the theme, or let it emerge only in the action, mistrusting any character who seems too wise. ### 8. Hedged Endings LLMs often produce endings that try to be **both things at once** — neither tragic nor triumphant, "ambiguous but not unsatisfying." This can read as genuine artistic ambiguity, but it can also read as the story being unwilling to commit, trying to please every reader. Human writers are usually willing to leave some readers cold in service of the ending being genuinely true to the story's logic. ### 9. Research-Forward World-Building LLMs, drawing on vast knowledge of mythology, tend to **show their work** — inserting mythological details in ways that feel more like demonstration than storytelling. A human who loves Greek mythology includes what feels alive to them right now, not what would prove they know the material. ### 10. Emotional Safety in POV LLMs tend to keep the narrative at a **comfortable emotional distance** — close enough to feel intimate, far enough to avoid genuine discomfort. The really human moments in fiction often come from the narrative being *too close*, uncomfortably inside a feeling, without the camera pulling back to give relief. --- # Deep Dive Critique of the Current Plan ## The Intention Section ### "Tone sits between Terry Pratchett and Madeline Miller" This is a very **LLM thing to say**. It sounds precise but it isn't — it's a way of invoking quality by association. More pressingly, it risks producing writing that tries to be two things and achieves neither. Pratchett's warmth comes from genuine love of his characters and deep comic timing built over decades. Miller's gravity comes from her willingness to sit in suffering without relief. Trying to do both simultaneously can produce a kind of **prestige-flavored mush** — it sounds like literary fiction without having the specific nerve of either influence. A human writer would probably name one of these and quietly let the other influence leak in, or not name either and just write the thing. ### The Sneakers as Symbol — Over-Planned The symbolic architecture of the sneakers is **too thoroughly theorised in advance**. "The gods who have made peace with the modern world wear sneakers. The gods who haven't are in trouble." This is a clean thesis. Clean theses make for clean stories that feel like parables rather than fiction. Human writers often start with a symbol and let its meaning surprise them during writing. The meaning of the sneakers should probably be **less settled** going in — it might shift chapter to chapter, or mean something the writer didn't plan, or resist meaning something specific at all. The plan already has: Demeter is barefoot (meaningful), Ares has worn-out Jordans (meaningful), Persephone has Converse with flowers (meaningful), Zeus is barefoot (meaningful), Hermes' glow (meaningful). It's **too schematic**. Every footwear choice has been given a reading. This is a diagram, not a story. It will read as a puzzle the author is guiding the reader to solve rather than a world the reader is discovering. ### "He is wry and world-weary but not cynical" This character description is **almost meaningless** — it describes the protagonist of approximately 60% of all urban fantasy. What is specific about Hermes? What are his *particular* blind spots? What does he find funny that he shouldn't? What does he refuse to examine in himself? What's embarrassing about him? "Wry and world-weary but not cynical" is a vibe, not a character. ### The Delivery Premise The delivery premise is clean, functional, a good genre scaffolding. But it's also **very safe**. It's the plot of many urban fantasy novels. A human writer might look at this and think: is there a way to make the actual plot weirder, more specific, less genre-familiar? The quest structure (collect four god-encounters, reach the final boss) is extremely legible. That legibility might be costing the story something. ### "Ambiguous but not unsatisfying" This is flagged above as an LLM tendency. The plan even states this explicitly as an intention. The ending as planned — Hermes disperses the belief-ember through the city, things are neither better nor worse, he gets back in his van — is **tastefully melancholy** in a way that might feel safe rather than true. It's the ending of a story that doesn't want to upset anyone. A human writer might ask: what ending would be *wrong* to some readers, genuinely uncomfortable, and yet the only true one for this story? --- ## Chapter-by-Chapter Critique ### Chapter 1 — *Wing Tips* The setup is competent. The concern: it asks the prose to do a lot of **table-setting** — here is Hermes, here is his job, here is his relationship to being divine, here is the package. This is the chapter most likely to feel like a Wikipedia entry in literary clothing. The "reflects briefly on what he is" is a red flag — this kind of expository self-reflection often reads as an author needing to deliver information. A human writer might start *later* or *stranger* — mid-action, mid-thought, somewhere unexpected that forces the reader to catch up. The ritualistic shoelace-tying detail is good — **concrete and specific**. But "the sneakers hum faintly" is the plan reaching for the magical too quickly and too easily. It announces the sneakers are magic before we've earned that reveal. ### Chapter 2 — *Green Thumb* Demeter at a farmer's market is a good image. But it's also a **predictable** image — goddess of harvest at a market is the most obvious possible placement. A human writer might have asked: where would Demeter be that I didn't expect? The conversation is described as "elliptical in the way old beings speak to each other" — this is a plan for dialogue that sounds wise and meaningful while actually risking saying nothing. Elliptical divine dialogue is a known literary cliché, especially in mythology-adjacent fiction. The pomegranate detail is doing very obvious mythological signalling. It's the Persephone symbol, and Demeter reaching for it reflexively is meant to be poignant. But it's **the most expected mythological callback** possible. A reader with any mythology knowledge will see it coming from a mile away. A human writer might discard this in favour of something stranger and more particular to Demeter as a character. ### Chapter 3 — *Iron* Ares as a boxing gym owner is also a **familiar archetype** — the war god in a combat sports setting appears in countless mythology-in-modernity stories. What is Ares doing that we haven't seen a war god do in fiction before? The chapter is described as "tense and kinetic" but the plan's description is mostly functional plot delivery: Hermes arrives, Ares detects the package, Ares lets him go but warns him. The detail that Ares is "good at coaching despite everything" is interesting — that's a human complication — but it's mentioned almost as an aside when it might be the most interesting thing in the chapter. The worn-down Jordans with separating soles is a strong image. But the explanation — "he hasn't bought new ones, he doesn't see the point" — over-explains it. The image should stand alone. ### Chapter 4 — *The Long Way* This is planned as an interior, reflective pause. Interior reflection chapters are **extremely difficult to write well** and are among the most common places for LLM prose to become abstract and thematic. "He thinks about the nature of belief — how it felt to be believed in completely" is a thesis statement waiting to happen. The risk here is a chapter that is all theme and no story, that tells the reader what to think about everything being set up. Human writers often externalise interiority — the character does something physical, strange, or small, and the thought happens *around* the action, not instead of it. What is Hermes *doing* in this chapter beyond sitting and thinking? A gyro is mentioned. Can more of the chapter live in that specific, physical, sensory world? ### Chapter 5 — *Perennial* Persephone at a florist/funeral home is clever — the underworld connection, the threshold between life and death. But again, it's a **thematically obvious placement**. The description of her as "the story's moral compass" is a significant concern. The wise, settled, peaceful female character who tells the male protagonist what he's actually feeling and what he should do is a trope — it's a kind of divine feminine wisdom-dispenser role that flattens her into a function. The line planned for her — "the choice belongs to the one who carries it, not the one who receives it" — is **exactly the kind of theme-statement** flagged as an LLM failing. It sounds like wisdom. It is actually the author telling the reader the plot. Human writers trust readers not to need this said aloud. The Converse with ballpoint flowers is charming and specific — that's a genuinely good detail. But is Persephone's settled happiness **too convenient**? She's the only god who's at peace, which makes her feel less like a character and more like a beacon. What is complicated about her? What is she not saying? ### Chapter 6 — *Interception* The action chapter. "Hermes in his element, moving through the city faster than he should be able to" — this is **told rather than shown**. The plan describes the scene's feel but doesn't suggest anything specific and surprising about how the chase actually unfolds. What makes this chase different from any other divine-figure-being-chased scene? The "minor deity or demigod running on fumes" antagonist is underdeveloped — this character exists only as a plot mechanism and it shows. The golden glow on the sneakers is the plan's most overtly magical moment, and it's been placed in the action chapter for maximum cool-factor. This is a slightly **cinematic, safe** choice — the hero's power manifests visually in the moment of crisis, like a superhero movie. It might be more interesting if the sneakers' power manifested in a way that was subtler, stranger, less triumphant. ### Chapter 7 — *The Address* Zeus as a tired old man in a modest house is a good image — avoiding the thunderous patriarch cliché. But "tired" is doing a lot of work in the plan without being made specific. What kind of tired? Tired like a man who has been waiting for a bus that isn't coming? Tired like someone after a long illness? Tired like someone who has made a decision and is now just waiting for it to happen? The specificity of his tiredness is the chapter. Zeus's plan — to use the ember to release the gods cleanly — is **philosophically interesting but emotionally safe**. He's not a villain. He just wants mercy-death for divinity. This is elegant, but it also means the story has no real antagonist, no genuine darkness — everyone is sympathetic, everyone is understandable. That's a very LLM moral structure. Where is the genuine moral danger in this story? The line — "You always did love roads" — is planned as the emotional peak of their exchange. It's a good line. But it might be the only genuinely unexpected beat in the whole chapter plan, which has otherwise described a scene that will unfold fairly predictably. ### Chapter 8 — *Delivery* The ending — dispersing the ember across the city — is **beautiful in concept** but potentially too neat in execution. The images planned (schoolyard, hospital, baseball diamond, laundromat) are the images of a story that wants to leave you feeling warm. They are chosen for their human, hopeful resonance. That's a choice, and it might be the wrong one. Is this the story's true ending, or the ending that makes the reader feel good? "This is neither tragedy nor triumph. This is what endurance looks like." — this is the author literally stating the theme. If this makes it into the prose (even reworked), it will flatten the ending. Humans feel this; they shouldn't be told it. The final image — sneakers on pedals, van in drive — is good. Clean, motion-forward, unresolved. That instinct is right. But everything before it in chapter 8 might be **over-explaining the choice** Hermes made and why it was right, which undermines the simplicity of the final image. --- # Brainstorming: How to Make It More Human ## On Character **Make Hermes more specific and more flawed.** What if Hermes is actually bad at one thing gods are supposed to be good at? What if he gets lost sometimes — genuinely, navigably lost — and finds this terrifying because he is the god of travelers? What if he actually cares too much about being liked and this is a problem? What if he's been avoiding Zeus not out of cosmic significance but out of something embarrassingly small — a slight, a petty argument, the divine equivalent of a family grudge? Small, embarrassing, specific motivations make characters human. **Give Persephone a complication.** Her peace shouldn't be total. Maybe she's settled but she misses something she won't name, and the flowers she draws on her sneakers are the thing she won't talk about. Let her be the moral compass without knowing she's being one. **Ares' coaching detail should be the whole scene.** The most interesting thing in the Ares chapter is that he's genuinely good with the kid he's coaching. What if the whole chapter is mostly just Hermes watching Ares coach — the interaction about the package happening almost in the margins — and the point is that Hermes sees something in Ares he didn't expect and doesn't know what to do with it? **Zeus should want something that makes Hermes uncomfortable for a reason we understand.** Not just "he wants to end it all peacefully." What if Zeus is right? What if Hermes knows he's right and can't bear to admit it? The moral complexity should cut Hermes, not just present a philosophical dilemma for the reader. ## On Plot **The delivery should go wrong in a smaller, more embarrassing way.** The interception chapter with the minor deity is generic action. What if the package gets lost for a mundane reason — left on a bus, or dropped when Hermes gets startled by something ordinary, or given to the wrong address by a genuine mistake — and he has to find it again? The comedy and the stakes can coexist without a chase scene. **Cut the "the package is warm and I feel a heartbeat" beats.** This is signalling meaning too hard. The package can just be a package. Its significance doesn't need magical-object cues. **Let one god encounter be genuinely unhelpful** — a god who doesn't give Hermes what he needs, who has no wisdom, who is just living their diminished life and isn't interested in being part of this story. That refusal to participate in the narrative would be very human and very funny. ## On Theme and Symbol **Let the sneakers mean different things in different chapters** rather than executing a predetermined symbolic grid. Let the meaning be discovered. Maybe in one chapter the sneakers are just uncomfortable. Maybe Hermes gets a blister. That small physical fact might say more than a golden glow. **Trust the story not to explain itself.** Cut or reframe any moment where a character states the theme. Let Persephone's wisdom be in what she *does*, not what she says. **The ending's distributed ember** — it doesn't need to be the warm, hopeful list of human spaces. What if one of the places the ember goes is somewhere surprising or uncomfortable? A parking lot at 2am. A police station. A casino. The randomness of dissipation is the point — it's not curated hope, it's just the last of something being released into a world that will do what it does. ## On Prose **Plan for some ugly sentences.** Deliberately. A chapter that starts mid-thought. A description that trails off. A piece of dialogue that doesn't land the way it was meant to. Human writing has texture and roughness; LLM writing tends toward a consistent polish that actually reads as artificial. **Find the embarrassing detail.** Every chapter should have one moment that is too specific, slightly awkward, not quite "literary" — the thing a careful editor might cut but that makes it feel real. What does Hermes eat in the van? What does the inside of the van smell like? What bad music does he have opinions about? **Let the mythology be in the background, not the foreground.** The less the story explains its mythological references, the better. Don't explain who Demeter is. Don't note that Hermes is the god of messengers when it's already obvious. Trust that the reader can feel the mythology without being shown it. **Vary the sentence rhythm radically between chapters.** Demeter's chapter should probably have longer, slower sentences — the rhythm of waiting and growing. Ares' chapter shorter, more percussive. Persephone's looser, more digressive. This is a prose-level differentiation that the plan doesn't currently address and that an LLM will tend to smooth over. ## On Ending **Consider whether Hermes should fail.** What if he actually delivers the package? And then sits outside in his van, and the lights in Zeus's house don't change, and he drives away not knowing what happened or whether he did the right thing? That uncertainty — not the warm dispersal through human spaces — might be the truer ending. The reader doesn't get to know. Neither does Hermes. **Or consider: he delivers it and nothing happens.** The ember was already spent. The gods have already been slowly going out for so long that the last light went out somewhere between Chicago and here, unnoticed. Zeus looks at the empty package and almost laughs. They sit with that. Then Hermes gets a new notification on his phone. Another delivery.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention ## What This Story Is Actually About A god who has gotten very good at not arriving. Hermes has spent centuries in motion — delivering, moving, connecting — because motion means you never have to stop somewhere long enough to feel what that place asks of you. The delivery to Zeus is the story of a man (a god) who has to arrive somewhere he has been avoiding, carrying something he doesn't want to hand over, and who discovers along the way that he has been more present in the world than he gave himself credit for. This is a story about avoidance, and about what happens when the road runs out. This personal centre — not "belief" as a grand theme, but the smaller, more embarrassing fact of a person who keeps moving because stopping is frightening — will shape every chapter. The mythology is the container. The feeling is the thing. ## Voice Close third person, past tense. The narrative sits very close to Hermes — close enough to be uncomfortable sometimes, close enough to catch him being petty or avoidant or quietly pleased with himself in ways he wouldn't admit. The voice is dry without announcing its dryness. It notices small physical things. It does not explain mythological context. It does not editorialize about theme. When Hermes thinks something important, it will tend to arrive sideways — in the middle of a sentence about something else, then gone. Sentences will vary by chapter, not by formula but by feel — some chapters earn longer, slower rhythms; others are clipped and restless. No chapter should sound quite like another. No character should sound like the narrator. ## The Sneakers The sneakers are Hermes' — grey Nike Air Max, iridescent in certain light, worn enough to look like any courier's shoes. They will not be explained. They will not glow at climactic moments. They will be noticed occasionally, the way you notice your own hands. Their significance will not be stated. Other gods' footwear will appear in the story as observed detail, without symbolic commentary attached — readers can make of it what they make of it. By the end, the sneakers will have accumulated meaning through repetition and small moments, not through a schematic plan. ## The Plot — Revised Hermes is a courier. He is assigned a package he recognises immediately — not because it's warm or humming, but because of the handwriting on the label, which he hasn't seen in decades and which he would know anywhere. The address it's going to is Zeus's. He knows this means something. He doesn't know what. He has three deliveries before Zeus: Demeter, Ares, and Persephone. He is not collecting information or wisdom. He is doing his job. What he receives from each of them is not clues or warnings but **contact** — the experience of being witnessed by someone who knows what he is. This turns out to matter more than any package. The package contains something that will not be revealed with fanfare. When Hermes gets to Zeus and they sit together, Zeus opens it and it contains almost nothing — something small, dry, ordinary-looking. Zeus is not surprised. Hermes realises he never knew what was in it. The delivery is just a delivery. The reason Zeus called him here specifically — not another courier, not a proxy — was to see him. That's all. That is the thing Hermes has been afraid of: being the destination, not the route. ## Characters — Revised **Hermes:** Specific flaws — he is late to things he pretends not to care about, he is genuinely terrible at receiving anything (compliments, help, concern), he finds Zeus embarrassing in the way adult children find parents embarrassing, he has been telling himself he doesn't mind his life for long enough that he's not sure it's still true. He is quick and funny and slightly cowardly about anything that requires stillness. **Demeter:** Not at the farmer's market. She works at a 24-hour laundromat she owns on the West Side. She is there at 6am when Hermes arrives with a package. The laundromat is full even at this hour. She knows all her regulars by name. She does not offer wisdom. She offers him a seat and bad coffee and asks how he's been in a way that makes clear she actually wants to know, which makes him uncomfortable, so he lies. **Ares:** Runs a boxing gym above the laundromat — not related to Demeter's business, just the building they both landed in. Hermes' second delivery. The scene is mostly Ares coaching a teenager. What makes Ares dangerous and also oddly moving is that he has found the exact right amount of force to apply to a fourteen-year-old learning to protect herself. He is good at this. Hermes doesn't know what to do with that. They don't talk much about the package. They don't talk much at all. **Persephone:** Florist on the North Side. She is not at peace. She is fine — there is a difference. She keeps drawing on her sneakers because she can't stop fidgeting. She talks a lot when she's nervous. She does not tell Hermes what the package means or what he should do. She tells him something about herself instead — something that isn't his business but that she says anyway — and in doing so makes him feel briefly, unexpectedly, like a person someone trusts. He leaves before he can figure out what to say. **Zeus:** Sixty-looking man in a quiet house. Not grand. Not theatrical. Just a man who called his son for a reason he hasn't explained yet, who answers the door like he wasn't sure anyone would come. ## What I'm Aiming For - Specificity over atmosphere. Concrete nouns. Objects. The actual smell of the laundromat, the actual sound of gloves on a bag. - Emotional content that arrives indirectly — in what Hermes doesn't say, doesn't finish, looks away from. - Gods who feel lived-in, not costumed. Their divinity is in texture, not in dramatic revelation. - Mythology assumed, not explained. Nothing is labelled. - An ending that doesn't resolve the central feeling — it just places it somewhere Hermes can carry it. - One moment per chapter that is slightly awkward, too specific, or unglamorous. ## What I'm Actively Avoiding - Any character stating the theme or delivering wisdom-speeches. - The sneakers glowing, humming, or performing visible magic at emotional peaks. - A chase scene or action setpiece — the interception chapter is cut. Tension will come from character, not from plot mechanics. - The list of warm human spaces at the end (schoolyard, hospital, etc.) — too curated, too reassuring. - Explanatory flashback or mythological footnoting. - A clean symbolic grid where every god's footwear has a predetermined reading. - Arriving at Zeus's house with any more gods between Persephone and the ending — the story is lean, three encounters, then arrival. - Telling the reader how to feel about the ending. --- # Chapter Planning ## Chapter 1 — *Manifest* (~1000 words) **What happens:** Tuesday, 5:47am. Hermes is at the WING dispatch hub picking up his manifest for the day — a converted warehouse on the Near West Side that smells like cardboard and someone's leftover Thai food. He knows every dispatcher by name. He likes the morning shift because the city is not yet fully itself and he can move through it without negotiating with it. We learn who he is through what he notices and what he ignores: he knows which traffic lights run slow, he knows three routes to anywhere, he cannot sit still while waiting. He picks up his packages. The last one on the manifest is flagged — not magically, just bureaucratically, a different colored sticker — and when he reads the label his handwriting recognition kicks in before his brain does and he is already halfway to a feeling before he decides not to have it. The address is in Pilsen. He knows the street. He puts the package at the bottom of his bag, under the others. He has three stops first. He goes. **The voice in this chapter:** Establishing. Comfortable. Slightly too comfortable — the voice of someone who has described their routine to themselves many times. Brisk, observational, with small moments where something is skipped over quickly. **Sneaker note:** He puts on his shoes in the van. This takes longer than it should. He doesn't think about why. --- ## Chapter 2 — *Wash, Dry, Fold* (~1000 words) **What happens:** 6:20am. Hermes delivers to Demeter's 24-hour laundromat on the West Side — a package of commercial detergent supplies, nothing cosmic. The laundromat is warm and loud with machines and a few people in the plastic chairs who are there because the laundromat is open and their apartment isn't warm enough. Demeter knows her regulars. She signs for the package with a pen she has to find at the bottom of a drawer. She gives Hermes bad coffee he doesn't want but takes. She asks how he is. He says fine, busy, you know how it is — the ritual deflection. She looks at him the way someone looks when they know you're lying but aren't going to push it. She shows him something small that's growing in a pot on the windowsill — a weed, technically, that pushed through a crack in the concrete outside and that she moved inside before the frost. She doesn't say anything about it. He can't tell if this is intentional or just a thing she wanted to show someone. He finishes the coffee, gets a signature, leaves. **The texture of this chapter:** Warm in a slightly fluorescent way — the warmth of functioning things, ordinary persisting. The conversation stays on the surface and the surface is enough. **The thing that stays with him:** Not a warning. Not wisdom. Just the way she said his name. It's been a while since anyone said it like that — like it was his actual name, not just what to call him. **Sneaker note:** He notices she's wearing clogs. The cheap foam kind from the pharmacy. This is observed and not interpreted. --- ## Chapter 3 — *Footwork* (~1000 words) **What happens:** 8:45am. Second floor of Demeter's building — same address, different suite, something Hermes finds either coincidental or cosmically lazy, he can't decide. Ares' gym, IRON & GLORY, hand-lettered sign on a door that doesn't lock properly. Hermes is delivering a box of jump ropes and some kind of nutritional supplement. Ares is in the middle of coaching a teenage girl on footwork — not fighting yet, just moving, weight distribution, where to put yourself in space so you don't get caught flat. Hermes waits. The girl is fourteen, maybe fifteen, all awkward seriousness. Ares is patient in a way that doesn't look like patience — it looks like he just has nowhere else to be, which amounts to the same thing. They exchange minimal words about the package. Ares signs the form. Before Hermes goes, Ares says something to him that isn't advice and isn't a threat — it's more like an observation, something he's noticed about Hermes over the years, said without particular weight, the way you mention the weather. Hermes doesn't have a response. He leaves, goes down the stairs, stands on the sidewalk for a moment longer than necessary. **The thing Ares says:** Not revealed in the plan — to be discovered in the writing. It should be something small and accurate that Hermes can't refute. **The texture of this chapter:** Kinetic but quietly so. The sound of feet on the mat. The particular smell of a used gym. Short sentences. No speeches. **Sneaker note:** Ares is wearing the same battered Jordans, sole separating at the toe. This is noticed because Hermes almost offers to — he doesn't know what. Buy him new ones? It's an absurd impulse. He doesn't mention it. --- ## Chapter 4 — *Mile Marker* (~1000 words) **What happens:** Between stops. Hermes eats a gyro in his van outside a parking garage on Michigan Avenue. The city does what cities do at 11am on a Tuesday. This chapter is interior but not abstractly so — the interiority happens through small physical particulars. He has the package from the bottom of his bag in his lap. He is not opening it. He is just holding it because it is there. He thinks about Zeus in fragments, not in clean retrospective — the fragments don't resolve into a thesis. A specific memory surfaces: something ordinary, not mythological, something in the register of a small human disappointment. He thinks about how he's been on every road and how Zeus, as far as he knows, hasn't left that house in Pilsen in years. He eats his gyro. A parking enforcement officer gives him a look. He pulls the van up ten feet to avoid the confrontation, which means he's now in a loading zone, which is somehow worse. He puts the package back in the bag. He has one more stop. **The function of this chapter:** Not a meditation on belief or divinity. A chapter about procrastination. About being in a van with something you don't want to deal with. The humour here is dry and accurate. The feeling underneath it is not. **Sneaker note:** His right shoe has a small stone in it that he's been ignoring since Ares' building. He takes it out. This takes four words and is not symbolic. --- ## Chapter 5 — *Perennial* (~1000 words) **What happens:** 1:30pm. North Side florist, narrow shop that sells flowers for weddings and funerals and also, somehow, for just buying. Persephone is behind the counter doing inventory, pen behind her ear, talking too fast when she sees him because she is, underneath her settled surface, nervous in a way she has decided to be honest about rather than conceal. She is glad to see him — this is also slightly alarming. The delivery is simple. What isn't simple is that she says something personal — not about the package, not about Zeus, not about any of it — something about herself, about what she's decided she can live with and what she's still arguing with herself about. It's too much information for a courier delivery. She knows this, which is why she says it quickly and then immediately starts talking about something else. Hermes receives this — the confidence, the trust — with his characteristic incompetence at receiving anything, which means he almost makes a joke and then doesn't, and just says *yeah* and nods, and somehow this is exactly right. He notices the flowers she's drawn on her sneakers. He almost says something about them. He doesn't. He goes. **The texture of this chapter:** Warmer, slightly more digressive, the chapter breathes a bit differently. Her voice when she talks is distinct from everyone else so far — faster, more associative. Not wise. Just honest. **What this chapter does for Hermes:** He gets to the van and sits for a second before starting the engine. He was just treated like someone worth telling things to. He is uncertain how to place this. **Sneaker note:** The drawn flowers on her canvas sneakers — he looks at them and doesn't say anything. The restraint of not commenting is a kind of tenderness he doesn't know he's performing. --- ## Chapter 6 — *Pilsen* (~1000 words) **What happens:** 3:15pm. Hermes drives to the address in Pilsen. This chapter is the approach — the drive, parking, the walk up to the house, the door. It takes longer than the distance justifies. He thinks about very little, deliberately. The neighborhood is observed closely — not romantically, just accurately, what's there. He finds the house. It is unremarkable in the specific way of houses that have been lived in by someone who doesn't particularly care about houses. He sits outside in the van for eleven minutes. The number is exact; he checks his phone. He gets out. He knocks. **The function of this chapter:** Delay, rendered honestly, without psychological over-explanation. The chapter is allowed to be about almost nothing — the way nothing fills up when you're trying not to think. The closer he gets to the house, the shorter the sentences get. This is not a structural rule but a felt thing. **The knock:** The chapter ends with the door opening. We do not yet see what's on the other side. **Sneaker note:** He looks down at his shoes on the doorstep while he waits. His feet are there. He is there. This is the simplest possible observation and it's enough. --- ## Chapter 7 — *The House* (~1000 words) **What happens:** Zeus answers the door. He is sixty-looking, wearing a thermal shirt and sweatpants, in his socks — the particular socks of a man who doesn't think about socks. He is not theatrical. He is not grand. He says *you came* in the tone of someone who was maybe forty percent sure. They sit in a kitchen. Zeus makes tea badly — he has never quite mastered tea, this is somehow consistent with everything. He asks about the route, the stops, whether Hermes saw Demeter, and these questions are genuine, not strategic. He has been wondering about her. The package is on the table. Zeus opens it with a pocketknife — the mundane precision of this matters — and inside is something small and dry and ordinary-looking. Hermes doesn't know what it is. Zeus seems to. They sit with it on the table between them. Zeus says, eventually, that he asked for the delivery personally because he didn't want to go himself and he couldn't send anyone he didn't trust and Hermes is — here he pauses — still Hermes. This lands strangely. They talk a little. Not about divinity or extinction or belief. About the city. About whether Demeter's laundromat is doing okay. About small things. The conversation is slightly awkward in the way of people who share a history and are speaking around most of it. **What isn't said:** Everything important. This is the chapter where the story trusts most in what isn't articulated. **Sneaker note:** Zeus in socks. Just socks, no shoes. This is the only footwear note in this chapter and it is delivered as pure observation. --- ## Chapter 8 — *Route* (~1000 words) **What happens:** Hermes leaves the house. The package is delivered. He has a signature. It is late afternoon, and the city has gone into its particular late-afternoon register — the light doing what it does over the lake in October, the traffic shifting. He drives. He has no more deliveries today. He drives anyway — not to anywhere, just through, the way you do when you're not ready to stop. He passes the laundromat. The lights are on. He passes the building with the gym — he can't see anything from the street. He doesn't stop at either. He drives. He thinks, finally, about what Ares said to him on the stairs — we hear it now, for the first time, and it is small and accurate and neither devastating nor redemptive, just true. He thinks about Persephone's flowers. He thinks about Zeus making bad tea. He gets on Lake Shore Drive and goes south and then north and then south again. At some point he pulls over and sits. His phone shows one new delivery on the manifest for tomorrow — early morning, West Side. He looks at his shoes on the pedals. He'll be there. **The ending:** Not a resolution. A resumption. Hermes will keep going because he is Hermes, but something is different in the quality of the going — not fixed, not transformed, just slightly less defended. The ending doesn't name this. It shows him looking at his shoes and it shows him accepting a delivery and it shows the city going on. That's all. **Sneaker note:** The last image is his shoes on the pedals. The iridescent sheen in the October light. He has not thought about it as significant. Neither should we be asked to.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Hermes ## Background, Personality, Idiosyncrasies, Flaws Hermes has been a courier in Chicago for eleven years, which is long enough that the dispatchers don't question his availability (always), his knowledge of the streets (uncanny), or the fact that he has never once been caught in a speed camera. He has had the same apartment in Wicker Park for nine of those eleven years — a one-bedroom he sublets from a woman named Carol who moved to Tucson and keeps meaning to sell but doesn't. The apartment is tidy in the way of someone who owns very little. He has a good coffee maker, bad art (three prints from a street fair he attended once and bought from because the artist looked cold), and a mattress on the floor that he keeps meaning to put a frame under. He is constitutionally incapable of being a passenger. If someone else is driving he watches the route with the focus of a man who suspects the driver is doing it wrong, and often they are, but this is not entirely the point. He is also bad at receiving: gifts, compliments, concern, apologies. When someone does something kind for him he tends to make a joke or change the subject, and if the kindness is substantial enough he will become quietly impossible to reach for a few days afterwards. He is aware of this. He has not fixed it. He is very good at his job and privately pleased about this in a way he would describe, if asked, as "yeah it pays fine." He knows the name of every overnight security guard on his regular routes. He remembers what people tell him — birthdays, the name of someone's sick dog, that a dispatcher's mother is visiting from Guadalajara — and produces these details later with a casualness that makes people feel obscurely looked after without knowing why. He does not know he does this. Or he knows and pretends he doesn't. His flaw, the core one, is avoidance dressed as motion. He is always on his way somewhere, which means he never has to be somewhere. He has maintained this at great personal cost for a very long time and has gotten so good at it that it no longer feels like avoidance. It just feels like his life. Trivial specifics: He has strong opinions about gyros and will go significantly out of his way for a good one. He cannot whistle — this frustrates him. He has started three books in the past year and finished none of them because he reads them in the van between deliveries and always loses them. He has a slight tell when he's uncomfortable: he looks at people's shoes. ## Physicality Medium height, lean in the way of someone who moves constantly rather than exercises deliberately. Dark hair that he cuts himself badly. Eyes that are very quick — the kind of eyes that have already noted the exits before you've finished shaking hands. He moves through spaces efficiently, not gracefully — there's a difference. He is not beautiful the way gods are supposed to be beautiful; he is striking in the way of someone whose face has more going on than you initially notice. He carries himself slightly forward, like he is always in the first half-second of walking somewhere. His hands are a courier's hands — slightly roughed, always doing something. When he stands still he tends to end up leaning against the nearest wall, weight on one foot, arms crossed in a way that is not hostile but is not exactly open. His default expression is a faint readiness, like he's waiting for the punchline. ## Motives, Allegiances, Relationships What Hermes wants, without knowing he wants it, is to be arrived at — to be someone's destination rather than the means of getting somewhere. He would find this formulation embarrassing and would reject it. His stated motive for today is to get through the manifest and get home. His real motive for today is to keep moving until he runs out of road, which he suspects, today, he might. He is genuinely fond of Demeter in the way you're fond of family you don't see enough because it makes you feel too much. He respects Ares in the way you respect someone you find limited but real. He is more rattled by Persephone than he will admit, for reasons he hasn't fully catalogued. Zeus is the person he has been not-thinking-about for eleven years, which is a form of thinking about someone constantly. ## Voice *From his internal monologue, stuck in traffic on the 290:* > The package was at the bottom of the bag. He wasn't checking. He knew it was there the same way you know a thing is in your pocket without touching it, the specific weight of something you'd rather not look at directly. Traffic moved. He moved with it. This was fine. This was Tuesday. *In conversation, to a dispatcher who asks if he minds the long route:* > "Mind it? No. Give me the long one. Short routes are for people who want to get somewhere." *(He says this like it's a joke. It is a joke. It is also the truest thing he'll say all day.)* *Noticing his own deflection after Demeter asks how he's been:* > She was asking actually, he could tell the difference. He said *fine* anyway, which was the verbal equivalent of locking a door he wasn't sure he'd remembered to put anything valuable in. --- # Demeter ## Background, Personality, Idiosyncrasies, Flaws Demeter has run the laundromat on West Madison for six years. Before that she did other things — a greenhouse in Michigan, a community garden project in St. Louis that got its funding cut, a period she doesn't talk about when she wasn't doing much of anything, which those who know her understand to be the years when Persephone was making her choices and Demeter had not yet made peace with them. She has mostly made peace with them. Mostly. The laundromat is called **CLEAN** in plain letters and she chose this name because she was tired of everything having a personality. She is there from 5am to noon most days, then one of two part-time employees takes over. The regulars know her. She knows them. She knows the kid who comes in every Sunday with six days of laundry because he can't face it any other way. She knows the woman who cries sometimes in the second chair from the window and has never asked her about it. She knows which machines run hot and which run honest. She is warm in the bone-deep way of someone for whom warmth is a form of intelligence, not performance. She is also exhausted in a way that has been going on long enough she's stopped registering it as exhaustion — it's just her baseline. She laughs easily and the laugh is real but it doesn't quite reach something behind her eyes that she keeps at a decent remove from most interactions. Her flaw: she gives and gives and then, occasionally, goes somewhere in herself that no one is allowed to follow, and this disappearance — which can be emotional rather than physical — frightens people who love her. She doesn't mean to do it. She does it anyway. Trivial specifics: She hates wasting food with a ferocity that is slightly disproportionate and will eat things long past the point of enjoyment rather than throw them away. She grows things in improbable places — not dramatically, just persistently: a tomato plant in a five-gallon bucket by the laundromat door, herbs on the windowsill in old soup cans, a fig tree in the back room that has no business being alive in Chicago and yet. She always knows what the weather is going to do, but she notices she's been wrong more often than she used to be, and this bothers her more than she lets on. She calls Persephone every Sunday. Persephone always picks up. ## Physicality She looks late fifties in the way that late fifties can look like a good outcome — face well-used, posture strong, grey threading through dark hair that she keeps pulled back practically. Her hands are the most notable thing about her: large and capable and always slightly dirty at the cuticles no matter how recently she's washed them, something perpetually agricultural in them. She moves with the deliberate unhurriedness of someone who has decided the pace at which they are willing to move through the world and is not interested in adjusting it. She has a stillness to her when she's listening that can feel like judgment but is actually just full attention, which is rarer and sometimes harder to be on the receiving end of. ## Motives, Allegiances, Relationships She wants the morning to go quietly and for the Hendersons to stop overloading machine four. More broadly she wants to know that things are growing — not in a grand way, just the ordinary miracle of persistence. She worries about Persephone not with the ferocity she used to but with a steady low-grade attention that doesn't have an off switch. She has complicated feelings about Zeus that she has refined, over centuries, into something that looks like indifference and functions as a kind of low-level grief. She has simpler feelings about Hermes — she's always liked him, he reminds her of something quick and necessary, like water finding its level. When Hermes comes through she is genuinely glad to see him and genuinely sad when he deflects, in that order, and she does not push because she has learned that pushing quick things only makes them faster. ## Voice *To a regular who apologizes for coming in so early:* > "Four-thirty is fine. Five is fine. Whenever. The machines don't care and neither do I." *What she doesn't say to Hermes, watching him leave:* > He was fine, he said. She watched him go and thought about the pot on the sill — the weed that had come through the crack, the one she'd brought in before the frost. She wasn't sure why she'd shown him that. She showed it to people sometimes, when she didn't know how else to say *look, things persist.* *To Hermes, about the bad coffee:* > "It's terrible coffee. I know it's terrible. I've been making it wrong for six years and at this point it would feel strange to fix it." A pause. "Sit for a minute if you want." --- # Ares ## Background, Personality, Idiosyncrasies, Flaws Ares has had the gym for eight years. Before that he moved around — security, demolition work, a period driving trucks long-haul that he remembers with something like nostalgia, the meditative quality of it, just road and weight and destination. He is not a man who reflects on whether his current situation represents a fall from former glory. He is a man who found a broken building, fixed it with his hands over the course of a winter, put a sign on the door, and started showing up. That is how he understands most things worth understanding. He coaches because fighters come to him and he is good at it and they need it — these are sufficient reasons. He is not running a legacy project or searching for meaning. He noticed that a teenage girl in the neighborhood was getting into the wrong kind of trouble and he talked to her mother and now she comes in at 7am on school days and hits the bag for forty-five minutes before the bus. This is not something he talks about. He would be annoyed if someone made it into a story. His personality is compact. He uses few words and the words he uses are direct, which some people find cold and others find enormously restful. He is not cold — he is someone who got burned enough times by his own volatility that he built a very sturdy wall around it, and behind the wall is something still large and hot that he manages through physical work and routine and very occasional bouts of watching nature documentaries late at night, which is the one private thing about him. His flaw: he has no language for damage that isn't physical. He can see a fighter's bad shoulder before they mention it hurts. He cannot see his own particular loneliness, which is the loneliness of someone who was made for something vast and is now applying that capacity to something small and good and insufficient. He is not unhappy. He does not know what he is. Trivial specifics: He eats the same lunch every day (rice, chicken, whatever vegetable was cheapest) and has done for years and sees no reason to vary this. He is surprisingly gentle with equipment — wraps a jump rope with care, checks the bag hooks, notices when something needs oil before it breaks. He cannot watch boxing on television; he finds it frustrating in a way he hasn't fully examined. He played chess for a period and was terrifyingly good at it and stopped when he won too many times in a row and it started to feel like something else. ## Physicality Large without being cartoonish — the kind of large that reads as just how the person is built rather than a project. He has the face of someone who has taken a hit and not particularly minded: slightly asymmetrical, a jaw like a shelf, small old scar at the corner of his left eye. He keeps his head close-shaved. He does not fidget, shift weight, or look away when talking to someone — not as a power move, just as his default. His voice is low and measured and he speaks slower than he thinks. He moves through the gym with total ownership of the space, not aggressively but completely, the way large competent animals move through their territory. Outside the gym he is more contained, like something that knows it's in a different environment. ## Motives, Allegiances, Relationships Ares wants the session to go well. He wants the girl — Maya — to get her weight right on the back foot before he has to tell her again. He wants the heating to hold for one more winter before he has to deal with it. These are today's wants. His relationship with Hermes is old and oblique — they have never been close, but they share the particular bond of beings who have seen the same long strange thing from different angles. Ares trusts Hermes in the limited way he trusts anyone: provisionally, based on pattern.
