OVERALL: This is accomplished YA fiction with a genuine voice, real psychological specificity, and good control of tone. Its weaknesses are minor: a slightly too-neat ending, occasional self-consciousness in the prose, and a failure to fully deliver on the plan's promise of specific rather than vague catastrophising. It is not amateurish. It is not purple. It earns most of what it reaches for. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Four — "The Actual Situation" The bathroom at Marcus Chen's — Ryan Chen's, Marcus's brother's, whatever — is small and smells of someone's good shampoo and has a print on the wall of the Amalfi Coast that's been hung slightly crooked and that I have been looking at for the past ten minutes while things have been resolving themselves, biologically, without my input or enthusiasm. I'm fine. I want to say that first. I'm completely fine. I came on about forty minutes ago, discovered this in the way that is always somehow a surprise no matter how many times it happens, and sent a text to Cass before I'd even finished processing the situation, which is just good crisis management. Except what I actually did, because my phone was already open and my thumb was already moving, was tap slightly too high in my contacts and send it to the person above Cass. Joel Marcus. Of all the — I've looked at this three times now, the sent message, and it's just sitting there being true. Three blood drop emojis delivered to Joel Marcus at 12:04am. I cannot explain what was in my head because what was in my head was: *Cass, emergency, blood,* in that order, rapidly, and my thumb made a decision that I wasn't consulted on. Cass is on the edge of the bath. She has produced, via her brother's girlfriend Sasha — who I've decided I would die for, situationally — the necessary supplies, and the situation is managed, and I'm fine, and I am looking at the Amalfi Coast print and thinking about Joel Marcus. "You've gone quiet," Cass says. "I sent the text to the wrong person." Cass looks up from her phone. "How wrong?" I show her. She reads it. Her face does several things. "Okay," she says, in the voice she uses when something is funny and she's deciding whether this is the moment. "Okay. Who's Joel —" "Don't." "I'm just asking —" "He's in my contacts from Year 9, there was a project, it was —" "Right." She hands my phone back. She has the specific Cass expression I've known since Year 7 — the one where she's already three steps ahead and is waiting with some patience for the rest of the world to catch up. "Has he texted back?" I look at my phone properly for the first time since I came into the bathroom. There are two texts from Joel. Both sent within the last hour. Both the same: *are you ok* — lowercase, no question mark, which is such a particular way to text that I read them both twice. Cass is watching me. "He texted twice," I say. "Both the same?" "Both the same." She makes a sound that's not quite a laugh. "What are you going to do?" This is the question. I look at the texts. *Are you ok* sitting there twice, identical, like he typed it out the second time rather than just copying the first, which means he thought about it twice separately, which means — I stop. I'm reading a lot into punctuation choices at 1:15am in a bathroom at a party I'm now not entirely sure why I came to. This is not my best analytical work. The thing about Joel Marcus is that he's difficult to have a simple opinion about, which I find annoying because I have quick opinions about most things and they're usually right. He's in my English class and he says things occasionally that make me want to know what else is in his head, which happens about twice a term and I don't do anything about it because — because it's Joel Marcus, and he looks at the middle distance after he says something interesting as if he's trying to pretend he didn't say it, and I don't know what you're supposed to do with that. He dropped my pen once. Picked it up and handed it back. Didn't make it into a thing, which I noticed, because most people make it into a thing. I know his number is in my phone. I know it didn't come out of nowhere. The Year 9 project is true — there was a project, we were briefly in a group together, he did three-quarters of the work without mentioning it, I noticed, we got a B+ — but I could have deleted the number afterwards, and I didn't. I've thought about why I didn't delete it roughly never, in the sense that I haven't sat down and formally thought about it, and roughly constantly, in the sense that it's a fact I'm aware of and have continued to be aware of. "Priya." "I'm thinking." "Your thinking face is very intense." "Cass." She raises her hands. "I'm just saying there's a boy who texted you twice at one in the morning when he didn't have to and you're doing your maths face about it." "I don't have a maths face." "You have a maths face." Outside the bathroom, the party is still happening. I can feel the bass through the floor tiles — something slower than earlier, the playlist doing what late-party playlists do. Someone knocks on the bathroom door, tries the handle, goes away. The Amalfi Coast print is still crooked. I should straighten it. I'm not going to straighten it. The thing I'm not saying to Cass, because saying it would require a longer conversation than I want to have in this bathroom at this hour, is that there's something about the lowercase that's got into my head. The way it reads. *Are you ok* — careful, and small, and real, in a way that I don't know how to categorise efficiently. I could text back. I could say: *wrong person, sorry, I'm fine.* Clean and done. He'd probably be relieved. Straightforward. "I'll sort it tomorrow," I say. I put my phone in my pocket. Cass looks at me in the way that means she has more to say and is filing it for later. "Sasha's getting chips," she says instead. "You want chips?" I do want chips. "Yeah," I say. "Come on then."
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is Chapter 4, Priya's first POV chapter, set in the bathroom at the party. The plan called for: Priya on the toilet lid, Cass on the bath's edge, the crisis practically resolved, a real conversation between them, Priya discovering she texted Joel, her complicated reaction to his texts, her decision not to reply yet, and ending on something Cass says that has nothing to do with any of this, leaving Priya fine. Let's assess how well this was executed. **Voice and Character Consistency** The plan specified Priya's voice as "shorter sentences, less interior, more sensory" and "more comfortable with silence." The chapter largely delivers this. Priya's narration is more clipped than Joel's would be, more decisive in its rhythms. The sensory details are present but restrained — the shampoo smell, the crooked Amalfi Coast print, the bass through the floor tiles. These are well-chosen and specific without being overwrought. Priya's character profile describes someone who "makes decisions about people quickly and revises them slowly," who is "withholding in a way that she justifies as privacy," and who has "strategic vagueness" about her feelings for Joel. The chapter captures this well. Her internal accounting of why Joel is in her contacts — the Year 9 project story she tells herself, the awareness that she could have deleted it and didn't — is exactly the kind of self-aware-but-not-fully-honest interiority the profile describes. The line "I've thought about why I didn't delete it roughly never, in the sense that I haven't sat down and formally thought about it, and roughly constantly, in the sense that it's a fact I'm aware of and have continued to be aware of" is genuinely good — it captures the strategic vagueness without announcing it. The pen-dropping detail is nicely specific and consistent with Priya noticing things Joel wouldn't notice (as the plan specified). The B+ on the group project, Joel doing three-quarters of the work without mentioning it — these are the right kind of details. **Dialogue** The Cass dialogue is strong. "Your thinking face is very intense" / "I don't have a maths face" / "You have a maths face" — this is real, it has the texture of a long friendship, and Cass's character (the one who's already three steps ahead, who finds the situation delightedly funny) comes through without being over-explained. The dialogue is economical and does character work simultaneously. The plan called for Cass's reaction to be "delight — this is Cass being real, not a function. Cass's delight is specific and a bit annoying." This is Chapter 4, not Chapter 6 (where Cass sees the text in the morning), so Cass here is discovering it in real time. Her reaction — the face that "does several things," the "Okay" in the voice she uses when something is funny — is appropriately restrained given the context. She's in a bathroom at a party, not over cereal the next morning. This is a reasonable adaptation. **Following the Chapter Plan** The plan specified: "Priya is on the closed toilet lid. Cass is on the bath's edge." The chapter has Cass on the bath's edge but doesn't specify Priya's position — she's in the bathroom, the situation is resolved, but the toilet lid detail is absent. Minor. The plan called for "a specific conversation — not about Joel, not about the text, just about something real between them, the kind of thing that gets said in small rooms late at night." This is largely absent. The conversation in the chapter is almost entirely about Joel and the text. The plan wanted the Joel discovery to interrupt something else; here, the Joel discovery is the whole chapter. This is a meaningful deviation from the plan. The plan specified: "The chapter ends on something Cass says that has nothing to do with any of this, and Priya laughs, and we leave her fine." The ending — "Sasha's getting chips. You want chips?" — is close to this, and it works. Priya doesn't laugh explicitly but the "Yeah, come on then" accomplishes the same emotional beat. This is well-executed. The plan also specified that Priya "is about to reply and then doesn't. This is not cruelty; it's 1:15am and she doesn't know what to say yet." The chapter delivers this — "I'll sort it tomorrow" — though the decision feels slightly more resolved than the plan's "she doesn't know what to say yet." The plan wanted more uncertainty; the chapter gives us Priya making a decision, which is consistent with her character but slightly at odds with the plan's intention. **What Works** The lowercase observation — "careful, and small, and real, in a way that I don't know how to categorise efficiently" — is excellent. It's Priya's voice (the "efficiently" is perfect for her), it mirrors what the plan said about her noticing the lowercase, and it does emotional work without announcing it. The "maths face" exchange is the chapter's best dialogue moment — it's funny, it's specific to this friendship, and it reveals character without exposition. The Amalfi Coast print returning at the end ("still crooked. I should straighten it. I'm not going to straighten it.") is a nice small touch — it's the kind of detail that earns its repetition. **What Doesn't Fully Work** The chapter is slightly too tidy in its emotional accounting. Priya's interiority about Joel — the pen, the B+, the contacts — is well-written but perhaps slightly too organized for 1:15am in a bathroom. The plan wanted something less fully articulated, and this is fairly articulate. The "strategic vagueness" is described rather than enacted at moments. The opening — "I'm fine. I want to say that first. I'm completely fine." — is a good voice note but the "I want to say that first" is a slight tell, a bit of self-conscious narration that draws attention to itself. The chapter doesn't quite achieve the "specific conversation" between Priya and Cass that the plan wanted — the one that has nothing to do with Joel. The plan wanted the Joel discovery to interrupt something else; here, the Joel discovery is the whole chapter from the start. This means we don't get the texture of Priya and Cass's friendship independent of the plot function. **Overall** This is a strong chapter — well-voiced, character-consistent, with good dialogue and some genuinely excellent lines. It follows the plan's spirit if not every letter. The main weaknesses are: the absence of the "real conversation" between Priya and Cass that predates the Joel discovery, and occasional moments where the interiority is slightly too organized for the circumstances. But these are relatively minor against what the chapter achieves. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Five — "I Don't Know What Is Wrong But Something Is" 2:03am. I'm at my desk. I got back twenty minutes ago, came in through the front door with the careful lock, did the stairs, took my coat off in my room and put it on my chair and sat down in it, which means I'm sitting at my desk in my coat like someone who hasn't fully committed to being home yet. My trainers are still on. My phone is in front of me on the desk, face up, next to my closed laptop, and I'm looking at it with the focused attention of someone who has decided not to look at it. Nothing. The walk back was forty minutes because I went a different way, longer, because I wasn't ready to be home yet and moving felt better than not moving. I came back through the park, which was empty and dark and fine — I'm not scared of parks at night, I'm scared of specific things that I can name and locate, not ambient darkness, which is one of the more useful data points about how my brain works. In the park I sat on a bench for a bit. I thought about texting Dom again and didn't on the grounds that Dom has already shown me everything he has to offer in this situation, which is a question mark. So it's just me and the phone and the desk and the coat. I open my laptop. Close it. Open it again. Here is the version of tonight that I know is probably true: Priya was at a party. She sent a text to the wrong person. She's been at a party all night and hasn't checked her phone, or has checked it and clocked the mistake and is mildly embarrassed and will sort it tomorrow. She is fine. She is at a party being fine, and I have walked fifty-five minutes total through Nottingham in light rain for no reason, and tomorrow I will think about this and feel the particular feeling I'm going to feel about it, but right now she's fine. I know this is probably true. I also know — and this is the part that's harder to explain, the part that doesn't respond to logic the way logic says it should — that knowing something is probably true doesn't touch the other thing. The other thing sits underneath the probable truth and is unaffected by it. This is not a new situation. I have been here before. Not about Priya. About other things. The worst one was my mum. I was twelve, she had a headache for three days, and I became — not worried, I want to be precise about this, it wasn't worry, it was *certainty* — I became certain that something was seriously wrong. I built it in my head with the attention to detail of someone constructing something load-bearing. I had a timeline. I had, at one point, a plan for who I would call, in which order, the logistics of it. I didn't tell anyone. I went to school both days and came home and looked at her across the dinner table and waited. She was drinking less water than she should have been. This was the entirety of what was wrong. By Thursday she was fine. I dismantled the whole structure in about four seconds and felt such a specific species of relief and shame mixed together that I still can't find a name for it. Relieved she was fine. Ashamed of the architecture I'd built. And underneath both of those, something I didn't have a word for at twelve and don't fully have one for now: the unsettling sense that being wrong hadn't actually disproved anything. Next time, my brain said cheerfully, I might be right. And then next time comes and the machinery starts again. I look at my phone. I think about texting Priya a third time and don't because two unanswered texts is already a quantity that requires explanation. Instead I open my laptop and look at Ryan Chen's Instagram again, which is an activity I could stop doing at any point and have chosen not to. I've been through most of it now. There's a photo from three weeks ago — eight or nine people at what looks like a barbecue, Ryan in the middle, various people I don't recognise arranged around him. I've been looking at Ryan Chen's face in these photos trying to read something in it, which is not how faces work, I know that, but the looking feels like doing something, and doing something feels better than the desk and the coat and the waiting. He looks, if I'm honest, fine. He looks like a twenty-two-year-old who has barbecues. This is not satisfying. I close Instagram and look at the ceiling. The thought I keep not-having is about Priya specifically, about the specific version of events that my brain has built and furnished and which I've been refusing to walk all the way into. I've been standing at the door of it for two hours. And I know — I know the way I knew with my mum — that I could walk into it and it would feel like certainty and not like fear, and I also know, with the part of my brain that can still observe the other part from a small distance, that that feeling of certainty is not the same thing as actually knowing. These two things are both true. Neither of them helps. What actually helps, slightly, is noticing how cold my feet are, which brings me back into my body in a specific and useful way. I take my trainers off. Then my coat. I hang the coat on the chair properly, because I'm home now, because this is home, because Priya is probably at a party being fine. I sit on my bed. I lie down on my bed with my phone on my chest. At some point — I don't track when — I stop looking at the ceiling. The phone doesn't buzz. My feet warm up slowly. The house is quiet in the way houses go quiet after 2am, a different quiet from midnight, more settled, like it's stopped waiting. The last thing I'm aware of thinking is that I'm still in my jumper. Then I'm not aware of anything.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong piece of interior fiction that largely succeeds on its own terms. Let me work through it carefully. **Voice and Character** The Joel voice is well-maintained throughout. The plan called for a voice that goes "short and plain" when tired or frightened, and the chapter delivers this — the opening paragraphs are clipped and observational, the sentences doing exactly what they need to without flourish. The tic of over-qualification is present ("I want to be precise about this, it wasn't worry, it was *certainty*") and feels earned rather than performed. The "presumably" habit mentioned in the plan doesn't appear here, but the chapter doesn't need it — the voice is consistent with the profile without being a checklist. The mum-headache memory is the chapter's centrepiece and it works. It's specific, it's not flattering to Joel, and it does exactly what the plan asked: it reveals the shape of his anxiety as something with history and form. "I had a timeline. I had, at one point, a plan for who I would call, in which order, the logistics of it" — this is good. It shows the anxiety as architectural, systematic, which is more unsettling than generic catastrophising. The line "Next time, my brain said cheerfully, I might be right" is genuinely good — the adverb "cheerfully" doing real work. **What the Plan Asked For** The plan specified: "Joel's anxiety has done this before. Not about Priya, but about other things. He has a memory he doesn't want of a time he was very sure something terrible was happening and was wrong. We don't get the full memory — just enough. It doesn't stop him worrying now." The chapter delivers this precisely. The memory is given enough detail to be real but not so much that it sprawls. The plan also asked for "the closest thing to a dark note" and "something that has form and history, that embarrasses him even as he thinks it." The shame-and-relief compound feeling is handled well: "Relieved she was fine. Ashamed of the architecture I'd built." The plan asked for the chapter to end with "a boy asleep at a desk, phone dark, coat on" — the chapter modifies this slightly (he moves to the bed, takes the coat off) but the spirit is preserved. The modification is actually better: him taking the coat off and lying on the bed is a small act of self-care that doesn't sentimentalise but does humanise. **Where It's Less Strong** The Ryan Chen Instagram section is the weakest passage. "He looks, if I'm honest, fine. He looks like a twenty-two-year-old who has barbecues. This is not satisfying." This is trying for dry comedy and lands it only partially — "a twenty-two-year-old who has barbecues" is a bit too composed, a bit too much like a joke that knows it's a joke. The plan warned against "consistent voice register" and this moment tips slightly into the charming-wit register when the chapter should be in its flatter, more frightened mode. The closing paragraphs are good but slightly over-written in their quietness: "a different quiet from midnight, more settled, like it's stopped waiting" — this is the chapter's most self-consciously literary moment, and it's not quite earned. The house-quiet-stopping-waiting metaphor is the kind of thing that sounds like a closing image rather than something Joel would actually think. It's not egregious but it's the one moment where the prose reaches for effect. **Dialogue** There is no dialogue in this chapter, which is appropriate — it's a solo interior chapter. The plan didn't call for dialogue here. No score penalty warranted. **Show vs Tell** The chapter is largely showing — the coat left on, the trainers still on, the specific logistics of the anxiety — but there are moments of explicit statement: "I know this is probably true" / "Neither of them helps." These are borderline: they're in Joel's voice and feel like his self-aware narration rather than authorial intrusion, but they do state rather than embody. On balance, the chapter earns its occasional directness because the surrounding material is specific enough. **Creativity and Surprise** The mum-headache memory is the chapter's most creative element and it's genuinely good — specific, not generic, and it reframes Joel's anxiety in a way that makes it more interesting than standard teenage worry. The "certainty vs. worry" distinction is sharp. The observation that "being wrong hadn't actually disproved anything" is the chapter's best line and captures something true about anxiety that isn't often articulated this precisely. **Overall** This is a confident, well-executed chapter that follows its plan closely and delivers on the character work. Its weaknesses are minor: one slightly over-composed joke, one slightly literary closing image. The emotional core — the mum memory, the two-things-both-true structure — is genuinely good work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Six — "What Actually Happened" The cereal Cass has is the expensive kind, the kind with actual fruit in it rather than the kind with shapes that are allegedly fruit-adjacent, and I'm eating it at her kitchen table at quarter past ten on Sunday morning wearing her older brother's football shirt as a pyjama top because I slept in my dress and it's now non-operational. Marcus is still asleep. Cass's mum is at Pilates. Barry, Cass's stepfather, made us both a cup of tea at nine, said *morning ladies,* and retreated to the living room with the Sunday paper, which is exactly the right amount of Barry. Outside it's the particular grey of a Nottingham Sunday in October — not threatening, not dramatic, just committed. The kind of grey that's going to be there all day and knows it. "The DJ was terrible," Cass says. She's on her phone, feet on the chair next to her, in the way she sits when she's fully comfortable somewhere. "The DJ was the worst DJ I've ever heard." "He played *Wonderwall.*" "He played *Wonderwall* twice." "The second time was definitely on purpose." She scrolls something. "What are you doing today?" "I need to go home and shower and then —" I stop. "And then?" I get my phone out. I've been not-getting-my-phone-out all morning with the discipline of someone who knows that getting their phone out will start a thing, and now I'm starting the thing. I open messages. There are Joel's two texts, still sitting there, still lowercase, still without question marks. I show Cass. She puts her own phone down, which is how I know she appreciates the significance — Cass putting her phone down is a real gesture, it means full attention is being allocated. She reads both texts. She reads them again. Then she looks at me with an expression that I recognise as the one she deploys when she is about to enjoy herself. "So," she says. "So." "Joel Marcus." "Yes." "Who is in your phone because of a Year 9 project." I eat a spoonful of cereal. "Priya." "I know what you're going to say." "I haven't said it yet." "You're going to say something about how it's funny, and then you're going to say something about how it's also sort of —" "It's sort of sweet," Cass says, simply, without performance. Not *it's sort of sweet* with a raise of the eyebrows, or *it's sort of sweet* with the weight of implication, just the words. This is why Cass is my best friend. She knows when to land a thing flat. I look at my phone. Here is the logic that I worked through last night before I fell asleep, that I woke up with already mostly assembled: Joel Marcus is directly above Cass in my contacts. *C* and *J* — no, wait. I go to my contacts and look. Cass is saved as *Cass,* obviously, which is under C. Joel is saved as *Joel M,* which is — also under J. That's not adjacent. I frown at this for a moment. Then I scroll up. He's not under J. He's saved as — I stare at this for a second — He's saved under *M.* Joel *Marcus.* I've saved him by surname. I put my phone face down on the table. "What?" Cass says. "Nothing." "Priya." "I saved him by surname." I look at the ceiling. "So he's between *Mum* and *Mr. Patterson.*" There's a pause. Cass does a thing with her mouth. "Mr. Patterson your geography teacher." "Yes." "Okay." She picks up her tea. "Okay." "I didn't do it on purpose." "I'm sure." "It's just how I —" I stop, because there is no good end to that sentence. It's just how I saved him, in Year 9, by surname, formally, as if he was a professional contact, which meant he ended up between my mum and my geography teacher and directly above Cass, alphabetically, and so when I needed Cass at midnight I tapped one contact too high and sent three blood emojis to Joel Marcus instead. The sequence of events is very clear. I pick my phone back up. I look at Joel's two texts. Both sent in the middle of the night — the first at 1:06am, the second — I check the timestamp properly for the first time — 1:43am. The second one was sent at 1:43am. That's thirty-seven minutes after the first. I do the maths on this without wanting to. *Are you ok* at 1:06. No reply. *Are you ok* again at 1:43. Thirty-seven minutes of silence from me and he texted again. "He went to the party," I say. Cass blinks. "What?" "Not — he didn't come in. I don't think." I'm working through it. "But he knew I was at Ryan's. Somehow. So he —" I think about Joel Marcus on Westdale Lane at some point in the middle of last night and something moves in my chest, slightly, which I note and move on from. "The second text was sent at 1:43. That's not from someone lying in bed. That's from someone who's been —" "Oh," Cass says. "Yeah." We sit with this. Outside a car goes past doing something unnecessary with its engine. Barry laughs at something in the living room, a single laugh, comfortable, Sunday-shaped. The cereal is getting soft in my bowl and I don't do anything about it. "So what are you going to do?" Cass asks. I've been thinking about this since approximately 1:15am. The options are: text him an explanation, which is not enough. Say nothing, which is worse. Or — "I'm going to call him," I say. Cass nods slowly, the way she does when something has confirmed a thing she already thought. "Today?" "Now," I say. "Before I think about it more." I pick up my phone.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong piece of YA fiction that largely delivers on its plan and demonstrates genuine craft. Let me work through its qualities and weaknesses systematically. **Voice and Character** Priya's voice is well-differentiated from Joel's. The chapter delivers on the plan's promise of "shorter sentences, less interior, more sensory." The opening paragraph — the cereal, Marcus still asleep, Barry making tea and retreating — establishes domestic texture efficiently without over-explaining. The Barry detail ("exactly the right amount of Barry") is a small, precise, funny observation that feels genuinely Priya rather than authorial. The contacts revelation is the chapter's centrepiece and it works extremely well. The plan called for Priya to discover Joel is saved by surname, and the execution is better than the plan described: Priya's initial confident logic ("C and J — no, wait") followed by the dawning realisation that she's saved him under M, formally, "as if he was a professional contact," is genuinely funny and genuinely revealing. The detail that he ends up between Mum and Mr. Patterson is the kind of specific absurdity that earns its place. Crucially, the chapter doesn't over-explain what this means — it lets Priya put her phone face down and move on, which is exactly right. The timestamp mathematics — 1:06, then 1:43, thirty-seven minutes — is handled well. "I do the maths on this without wanting to" is a good line. The inference that the second text wasn't sent from bed is Priya being competent in the way her character profile demands: she reads evidence, she draws conclusions, she doesn't sentimentalise. **Cass** Cass is rendered faithfully to her profile. The phone-down gesture as a marker of genuine attention is a nice specific. "It's sort of sweet" delivered flat, without performance, is exactly what the character profile promised: "she knows when to land a thing flat." The dialogue between them has the texture of a real long-term friendship — the shorthand, the unfinished sentences, the comfortable silences. Cass's "Okay. Okay." after the Mr. Patterson revelation is perfectly calibrated. **What the Chapter Does Well** The chapter follows its plan closely and improves on it in places. The plan called for Priya to "walk through the logic" of the contacts situation, and the execution makes this more dramatically interesting by having Priya get the logic wrong first (thinking it's C vs J) before discovering the more embarrassing truth. This is better than the plan. The ending — "Before I think about it more" — is appropriately decisive and character-consistent. Priya makes decisions to resolve ambiguity; this is her doing that. The "something moves in my chest, slightly, which I note and move on from" is a good line. It's honest about the feeling without dwelling on it, which is exactly Priya's mode. **Weaknesses** The chapter is perhaps slightly too smooth. The plan called for Priya's mortification to be "as real as Joel's" and while there's embarrassment here, it's managed embarrassment — Priya is in control of it throughout. The chapter doesn't quite find the moment where she's genuinely uncomfortable rather than wryly self-aware. The plan's voice sample ("I'm not embarrassed about the — I'm embarrassed about the other part. That he went. Why did he go, Cass.") is more raw than anything in the actual chapter. The grey October Sunday description ("not threatening, not dramatic, just committed") is the chapter's one moment of slightly self-conscious writing — it's trying a bit hard to be a good sentence. "The kind of grey that's going to be there all day and knows it" is fine but edges toward the kind of weather-as-mood-signalling that the plan explicitly wanted to avoid. The plan mentioned that Cass's delight should be "specific and a bit annoying" — the chapter gets the delight but softens the "bit annoying" quality. Cass is almost too perfectly supportive here. The plan's Cass was meant to have a quality of enjoying the drama slightly more than is strictly kind; that edge is present but muted. The Wonderwall exchange at the opening is charming but slightly disconnected from what follows — it establishes the friendship register well but the transition into the phone revelation feels slightly abrupt. **Dialogue** The dialogue is generally strong — naturalistic, character-specific, not over-written. The "He went to the party" / "What?" exchange is well-handled. No line feels like it exists to deliver information rather than to be what a person would say. **Overall** This is confident, well-crafted chapter work. It follows the plan faithfully, renders both characters accurately, and finds genuine comedy in the contacts revelation without over-milking it. Its weaknesses are of smoothness rather than failure — it's slightly too controlled, slightly too comfortable, and misses the rawer edge the plan promised. But these are minor criticisms of a chapter that does most of what it sets out to do with real skill. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Seven — "Oh" I'm asleep at my desk when my phone rings. I know I'm asleep at my desk because when the phone rings I'm already mid-sentence in a dream about something I immediately lose, and then I'm awake, and there's a keyboard imprint on my left forearm, and the light in my room is wrong — still dark outside, but a different dark than last night, the kind that means it's morning rather than very late. My phone is ringing. I pick it up. The screen says *Unknown Contact.* I answer it. This is not a decision. This is my thumb moving before I have any say in the matter. "Hello," I say. Or something like hello. I'm not fully sure it's a word. "Joel." A pause. "It's Priya." There's a version of this moment where I say something good. Where I'm awake enough to respond like a person, with a sentence that has a shape to it, something that lands okay. I'm not in that version. In the version I'm actually in, I say: "Right, yeah, I —" and then don't finish the sentence, because I've realised I'm still in my jumper from last night and also that my mouth is only partially working and also that Priya Okafor-Singh is on the phone, which is a combination of factors that my just-woken brain cannot organise into output. "Sorry," she says. "Is this — are you awake?" "Yes." I sit up straighter. The keyboard imprint on my arm itches. "Yeah, sorry, I was just —" "I woke you up." "No." Beat. "A bit." A pause. Not uncomfortable, I don't think. I'm not calibrated enough yet to read pauses properly but it doesn't feel like she's about to hang up. "Okay," Priya says. "So. I need to tell you something about last night." I wait. "I sent that text to the wrong person." I wait. "I meant to send it to Cass. She's directly — well. You're in my contacts and you were above her, it was an accident, I just —" She pauses. "I'd come on. At the party. I needed Cass. I wasn't — nothing was wrong." The room is quiet. Outside a bird is doing something confident and inappropriate for the hour. My desk has my closed laptop on it and a mug with a pen in it and last night's phone, and I'm looking at all of this while my brain performs a rapid structural reassessment of the last eight hours. Nothing was wrong. She'd come on at the party and needed Cass. Three blood drop emojis. "Oh," I say. "Yeah." "Okay." I'm nodding, which she can't see. I stop nodding. "Okay, no, that — that makes sense, I'm glad you're —" "I'm really sorry," she says. "I didn't realise until this morning when I saw — I didn't check my phone properly last night and then this morning I looked and you'd —" "It's fine," I say. "Genuinely. It's fine." Another pause. The thing happening inside me right now is not simple. There's relief, which is real — she's fine, she was always fine, the thing I built in my head last night was wrong in the way I knew it was probably wrong, and the relief at being wrong is the specific complicated relief I've felt before. But there's something next to the relief that I wasn't prepared for. I've been awake for — I don't know, eight hours of last night, most of it arranged around the fact of her three emojis, and now the emojis mean something small and biological and accidental, and the restructuring required is significant. I'm not going to say any of this. "I went to Chen's," I say instead. I don't know why I say it. It's just: true, and present, and my mouth says it before the rest of me can object. Silence. "You went to Chen's," Priya says. "Yeah." "Last night." "About — yeah. About half one." The silence this time is a different length than the previous silences and has a different texture. I can't read it. I'm awake enough now to know that I want to read it and not awake enough to do it. "I didn't go in," I say, in case this matters. "I just — I walked there. I stood outside. I was going to —" I stop, because the end of that sentence is *I was going to make sure you were okay,* which is accurate and also sounds like something I'm not sure I'm allowed to say plainly. "I walked back after." Another pause. "How long did you stand outside?" she asks. Her voice has shifted, I think. I can't tell to what. "A bit," I say. "Not — it wasn't. A bit." The bird outside is still going. My room is getting lighter in the way it does at this time of year, reluctantly, like it's not sure it can be bothered. I look at the keyboard imprint on my arm. "Joel." "Yeah." "That was —" She stops. "I know," I say. "I was going to say —" "I know how it sounds." "I was going to say that was —" She stops again and I hear her exhale, which sounds like making a decision. "I've got to go, my mum wants —" another pause, shorter, "that was a lot. What you did. I just — yeah." I look at the wall. "Okay," I say. "Okay." And then: "I'll see you Monday." Not *see you around.* Not *take care* or *anyway.* *I'll see you Monday,* with the weight distributed toward *Monday* in a way that might mean something, in a way I will spend the rest of Sunday thinking about whether it means something. "Yeah," I say. "See you Monday." The call ends. I put the phone down on the desk. I sit in the grey Sunday morning light in my jumper from last night with the keyboard imprint fading on my arm. The bird outside finishes whatever it was saying. I don't move for a while. Then I get up and go downstairs and make toast, because I haven't eaten since yesterday and the body continues to require things regardless of circumstances, which is its own kind of dark comedy.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter Seven is a remarkably controlled piece of writing that demonstrates genuine craft in its handling of revelation, anticlimax, and the specific texture of adolescent emotional experience. Let me assess it across the relevant dimensions. VOICE AND CHARACTER Joel's voice is consistent and earned throughout. The plan called for sentences that go "short and plain" when he's tired or frightened, and the chapter delivers this precisely: "Right, yeah, I —" trailing off, "A bit," "Yeah," the repeated "Okay." The over-literacy is present but not deployed as charm — he reaches for frameworks and they don't quite arrive. The self-awareness that doesn't help him is rendered accurately: "I'm not going to say any of this" followed immediately by saying exactly the thing he wasn't going to say. This is psychologically true and executed without announcement. The plan specified that Joel says "I went to Chen's" without knowing why, and the chapter handles this beautifully — it arrives as a genuine surprise within the narrative, the mouth moving before the brain can object, and the reader feels the rightness of it even as Joel doesn't fully understand it. This is showing rather than telling at its best. Priya's voice in the phone call is appropriately brief and slightly guarded — "I was going to say that was —" stopping twice, the exhale "which sounds like making a decision." The plan called for her to say something "less articulate and more real" than a composed declaration, and "that was a lot. What you did. I just — yeah" achieves this. It's not eloquent. It's true. DIALOGUE The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. It's written in the way people actually talk when they're managing something emotionally significant — incomplete sentences, false starts, pauses that carry weight. "How long did you stand outside?" is a perfect question: it reveals Priya's interest without declaring it, and Joel's answer ("A bit. Not — it wasn't. A bit.") is exactly the kind of response that communicates more than it says. The plan called for "something passing through the call that neither of them picks up or puts down" and this is achieved without being labelled. The "I'll see you Monday" ending to the call is well-handled. The plan specified this weight distribution toward "Monday" and the chapter earns it by having Joel notice it and then immediately qualify his noticing — "in a way that might mean something, in a way I will spend the rest of Sunday thinking about whether it means something." This is the right register: not certainty, not dismissal, but the specific suspended state of fifteen. STRUCTURAL EXECUTION The chapter is short, as planned, and the shortness is the point. The plan called for revelation scenes to happen fast, and this one does — the information is delivered in the first third, and the rest of the chapter is the aftermath of the information, which is where the real content lives. The restructuring Joel undergoes is rendered through specific physical details (the keyboard imprint, the bird, the grey light) rather than through interior declaration. The toast ending is excellent — "the body continues to require things regardless of circumstances, which is its own kind of dark comedy" — and it earns the lightness because it's been preceded by genuine emotional weight. It's also consistent with the plan's instruction to avoid closing images that do thematic work too visibly; this one does thematic work but through the mundane rather than the symbolic. WEAKNESSES The bird is used twice — "doing something confident and inappropriate for the hour" and then "finishes whatever it was saying" — and while neither instance is bad, the second callback is slightly too neat. The plan warned against paying off every detail, and the bird's return is a small instance of exactly that tidiness. "The room is quiet. Outside a bird is doing something confident and inappropriate for the hour" — "confident and inappropriate" is good, but "for the hour" is slightly weak as a qualifier. It's not wrong, it just doesn't quite land with the precision the rest of the prose achieves. The line "my brain performs a rapid structural reassessment of the last eight hours" is slightly more polished than Joel's voice warrants at this moment — it's the kind of formulation he might use when he has bandwidth, and the plan specified that when he's tired the sentences go short and plain. This is a minor inconsistency. The plan called for "something next to the relief that I wasn't prepared for" to be rendered with some specificity — the particular shape of Joel's anxiety, the embarrassment of having been wrong in a particular way. The chapter gestures at this ("the specific complicated relief I've felt before") but doesn't quite deliver the promised edge. The plan said "what he imagines has happened to Priya reveals a particular shape to his anxiety — something that has form and history, that embarrasses him even as he thinks it." Chapter Five presumably handled this more directly, but the callback here is slightly vague where it could be more specific. OVERALL ASSESSMENT This is genuinely accomplished YA fiction writing. The chapter follows its plan closely, executes the voice consistently, handles the revelation with appropriate speed and emotional complexity, and ends in the right register — slightly smaller than the reader might want, in the right way. The dialogue is the best element: it sounds like real people managing real feelings in real time. The weaknesses are minor and don't undermine the chapter's success. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Eight — "Monday" Monday arrives the way Mondays do, which is without asking. I'm up at seven, which is earlier than necessary, for reasons I'm not going to examine. I shower. I look at my phone. Nothing, which is fine, which is what I expected, which is completely fine. I eat toast standing up in the kitchen while my mum moves around me in her pre-work pattern — kettle, radio, keys, the particular sequence of a person who has done the same morning enough times that it runs without input. She asks if I'm alright. I say yeah. She looks at me for a second in the way she does occasionally, the look that means she's clocked something and filed it rather than pursued it, and then she's back to the sequence. I leave at eight-fifteen. The street is cold, properly cold, the first real cold of October, the kind that gets into your collar. I walk to school the way I always walk to school — through Woodthorpe, which today is just a route and not a thing I'm thinking about. --- School is school. This is both the problem and the solution. First two periods are maths and then geography, which require enough of my brain that I don't have full capacity for other things. This is useful. Mr. Patterson does something with contour lines that requires concentration and I give it concentration and for forty minutes I'm just a person in a geography lesson, which is — fine. Good, actually. Normal is underrated. Dom finds me at break. He's eating something from a packet and offers me some without looking at it first, which is Dom's version of hospitality. "You look better," he says. "Than what?" "Than Friday. You looked terrible Friday." "That was two days ago." "You still looked terrible." He eats something. "Farida was asking where you sit in English." I look at him. "Farida from our year?" "Yeah." "Why does Farida want to know where I sit in English?" Dom shrugs in a way that suggests he has theories and has decided they're not his to share. "Dunno. Told her second row, left side." I think about this for a moment and then stop thinking about it because I don't have the bandwidth today for more than one thing. "Thanks," I say. "Yeah." He offers the packet again. I take something. It's a prawn cocktail crisp, which I don't particularly like, and I eat it anyway because it's there and Dom offered it and some things are simple. --- I see Priya twice before lunch. The first time is in the corridor outside the science block. She's with Cass, both of them moving fast, Cass talking about something with her hands. Priya sees me. There's a moment — not long, maybe two seconds — where we both make a small adjustment, the kind of adjustment that people make when they've spoken on the phone and now there's a physical version of the other person to account for. She does something with her expression that isn't quite a smile, more an acknowledgment, and I do something back that I hope reads similarly. Then they're past. Cass, just before she's past, gives me a look. The look is not unkind. It is, if anything, appraising, in the way of someone checking whether a thing they've decided is true is in fact true. I don't know what conclusion she reaches. She's gone before I can tell. The second time, Priya is talking to Mrs. Okafor — not her mum, different Okafor, the chemistry teacher — outside the chemistry lab. I walk past without slowing down. She doesn't see me or doesn't indicate that she sees me, which is its own kind of data that I decide not to analyse. --- Lunch. I'm at my usual table in the canteen — not my usual table because I've claimed it or because it has any official status, just the table I end up at through the path-of-least-resistance that governs most of my movement through school. Dom is there already, talking to someone from his form about something I don't have context for. I put my tray down. I sit down. I open the book I've brought because I sometimes read at lunch, which is the kind of thing I do that I've stopped being embarrassed about. I read approximately one page. The canteen is loud in its usual way — the particular acoustics of a large room full of fifteen-year-olds produce a sound that's not quite noise and not quite conversation, just a general warm pressure. Someone drops something metallic near the serving hatch. The smell is today's hot option, which is something with mince. I'm looking at my book when Priya sits down. Not across the table. Next to me — the seat directly next to mine, which requires a specific choice to get to, which means she walked to this side of the table rather than the other side. She puts a container down, the kind she brings from home, and opens it without fanfare. Something with rice. Dom looks up, registers the situation, looks back down at his own phone with the elaborate incuriosity of someone pretending not to notice. "Hi," Priya says. "Hi," I say. She eats. I don't eat, for a second, and then I eat, because not eating would be strange. "How was your morning?" she asks. "Contour lines," I say. "Patterson?" "Patterson." She makes a sound of recognition and sympathy. We eat. It's slightly stilted in the way of two people who have agreed to be somewhere together without fully negotiating the terms. It's also — and I notice this with the part of my brain that notices things — fine. Actually fine. We talk about the contour lines, which is an unpromising subject, and then about something Cass said this morning that apparently annoyed Priya on a technicality, and I say something in response that I think lands okay, and she does something with her expression that's more than the acknowledgment from the corridor. At one point I make a reference that's too specific — a thing I do, a thing I've always done — and it doesn't land right, and there's a small horrible beat where it sits between us wrong, and Priya just looks at me for a second, and I think: *there it is, there's the part where this stops —* And she says: "What does that mean?" Not *what does that mean* dismissively. Just: asking. So I explain it. And she listens, with the stillness I didn't know I'd noticed about her until now, sitting right next to it, and at the end she says "okay, that's fair enough, actually," and we move on. Near the end of lunch, she says: "You should have just come. On Saturday." I look at my tray. "I wasn't invited." "I know." A pause — short, not loaded, just a breath. "I know you weren't." Which is not *I'm saying you should have come anyway.* Which is not a declaration or a resolution or a line that does the work of ten lines. It's just: *I know you weren't.* Sitting there simply, with Priya looking at her container of rice, and what it means or doesn't mean is not something I know yet, and possibly not something she knows yet either, and that's the shape of things right now. The bell goes. We leave the canteen separately because we have different afternoon lessons, which is just a fact about the timetable. At the door she goes left and I go right and that's that. --- I walk home at four through Woodthorpe, which is just a route. At some point between the park and Porchester Road I get my phone out and go to contacts and find *Joel M* — my own contact, that she showed up as in her phone, which she told me in the call, which I haven't stopped thinking about since — and I think: she saved me by surname. Formally. Like a geography teacher. I scroll to where I have her: *Unknown Contact.* Which is not right, not anymore, probably hasn't been right for a while if I'm honest about it. I press edit. I type: *Priya.* I look at it. I don't type anything after it. I save it and put my phone back in my pocket and walk the rest of the way home. The street is the same street. The cold is the same cold. My mum's car is in the drive, which means she's home early, which means she'll ask about my day, and I'll say fine, and she'll look at me with the look, and maybe today I'll say: *actually, it was alright.* The front door. The key. The familiar sound of the lock. Inside is warm.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a genuinely accomplished piece of YA fiction that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through it carefully. **Voice and Execution** The Joel voice is well-sustained throughout. The dry, self-aware register is present without being consistently clever — it goes flat when flatness is appropriate ("School is school. This is both the problem and the solution.") and sharpens when Joel has bandwidth for it. The tic of over-qualification is present ("which is fine, which is what I expected, which is completely fine") without being overdone. The "presumably" habit from the plan doesn't appear, but the spirit of it does. The voice feels inhabited rather than performed. The chapter earns its length. The structure — morning, break, two corridor sightings, lunch, walk home — is genuinely the texture of a school day rather than a highlight reel, which was the plan's explicit goal. The canteen scene doesn't rush to its emotional content; it earns it through the contour lines conversation, the prawn cocktail crisp, Dom's elaborate incuriosity. **The Lunch Scene** This is the chapter's centre and it works. The moment where Joel's too-specific reference doesn't land, and he braces for the end, and Priya simply asks "What does that mean?" — not dismissively, just asking — is exactly the kind of small, real moment the plan called for. It's not a declaration. It's two people establishing that talking is something they can do. The plan specified this precisely and the execution matches it. Priya's line — "You should have just come. On Saturday." / "I know you weren't." — is handled well. The plan explicitly rejected "I know. I'm saying you should have come anyway" as too composed, and what's here is less composed and more real. The ambiguity is genuine: "what it means or doesn't mean is not something I know yet, and possibly not something she knows yet either" — this is Joel's voice, not the author's, and it's the right note. **The Ending** The phone contact detail — *Unknown Contact* changed to *Priya* — is a good ending. It's small, specific, and does thematic work without announcing it. The plan said "not a closing image with symbolic weight" and this walks that line well: it has weight, but it's earned through specificity rather than symbolism. The final image of the warm house is perhaps the one moment where the writing reaches slightly for resonance ("Inside is warm") but it's brief and not overworked. **Weaknesses** The Cass look in the corridor — "appraising, in the way of someone checking whether a thing they've decided is true is in fact true" — is slightly over-explained. The reader doesn't need the full gloss on what the look means; the look itself would suffice. The Farida subplot (Dom mentioning Farida asking where Joel sits in English) is a nice touch from the character profiles but it's introduced and then explicitly dropped ("I decide not to analyse"). This is honest to Joel's character but it does feel slightly like a thread that was included and then abandoned rather than deliberately left open. The mum's "look that means she's clocked something and filed it rather than pursued it" is good characterisation but the final paragraph's "maybe today I'll say: actually, it was alright" is the one moment where the chapter tips toward sentimentality — it's slightly more resolved than the rest of the chapter's register. **Dialogue** The dialogue is strong. It's sparse and functional and doesn't over-explain. "Contour lines." / "Patterson?" / "Patterson." is exactly right. The lunch conversation is appropriately stilted without being awkward in a way that would undermine the scene. **Consistency with Plan and Profile** The chapter follows its plan closely. The two corridor sightings, the lunch scene, the slightly-misfiring moment, Priya's line that doesn't fully land as a declaration but is one, the walk home — all present and executed. Joel's passivity, his noticing, his self-awareness that doesn't help him — all consistent with the profile. Cass's appraising look is consistent with her character. Dom's prawn cocktail crisp hospitality is exactly Dom. **Overall** This is a chapter that does what it sets out to do with skill and restraint. It doesn't overreach. The comedy is present but not performed. The emotional content is real but not announced. The ending is smaller than the reader might want, in the right way. The main weaknesses are minor: one over-explained look, one slightly sentimental final beat, one dropped thread. These are small costs against a chapter that largely succeeds at being genuinely good rather than impressively showy. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is a genuinely accomplished piece of darkly comic British YA fiction that demonstrates considerable craft. Let me assess it systematically. STRENGTHS: The structural conceit is elegant and well-executed: we follow Joel's anxious midnight vigil in chapters 1-3 and 5, then pivot to Priya's perspective in chapters 4 and 6, before reuniting them in chapters 7-8. The reveal that the blood emojis were a period emergency sent to the wrong contact is perfectly calibrated for the genre — darkly comic, deflating Joel's catastrophizing without mocking it, and grounded in genuine teenage experience. The voice is the piece's greatest achievement. Joel's narration is distinctive, self-aware without being precocious, and consistently maintained. The parenthetical self-corrections ("Not specifically not invited. Just — the invitation didn't happen in my direction, which is different. Technically."), the methodical anxiety-cataloguing, the way he notices his own irrationality while being unable to stop it — all of this rings true. The Bamber Gascoigne detail is a lovely piece of British specificity that grounds the world immediately. The anxiety subplot — Joel's recognition that his catastrophizing has a pattern, the memory of his mother's three-day headache — is handled with real sensitivity. It avoids both pathologizing and dismissing his mental state, which is a difficult balance. The line "Next time, my brain said cheerfully, I might be right" is genuinely good. Priya's chapter (4) is well-differentiated in voice — more decisive, more socially embedded, with a different relationship to her own interiority. The Cass dynamic feels authentic. The revelation that Joel is saved between "Mum" and "Mr. Patterson" is a genuinely funny and revealing detail. The dialogue in chapters 7 and 8 is the piece's most technically demanding section and largely succeeds. The phone call is appropriately awkward, the pauses are doing real work, and "I'll see you Monday" landing with weight on "Monday" is a nice understated romantic beat. WEAKNESSES: The pacing in chapter 5 is slightly indulgent — it retreads the anxiety material from chapters 1-3 without adding enough new dimension. The mother's headache memory is important but could be tighter. Some of the self-conscious prose occasionally tips into affectation. "Expensive dark" is a phrase that wants to be clever but doesn't quite earn it. "Sunday-shaped" (applied to Barry's laugh) is the kind of compound adjective that sounds good but means little on examination. These are infrequent enough not to be a serious problem. The world-building is thin but appropriately so for the genre — Nottingham is gestured at rather than built, which works for a first-person teenage narrator but occasionally feels like an excuse not to do the work. The final chapter's resolution is perhaps slightly too neat — the canteen scene moves from stilted to comfortable a little quickly, and "I know you weren't" doing the heavy lifting of the romantic suggestion is almost too subtle, risking reading as nothing rather than something. However, this restraint is also the piece's ethos, so it's defensible. The "darkly comic" element of the prompt is present but could be pushed further — the comedy is gentle rather than dark, and the darkness (Joel's anxiety) is handled with more earnestness than comic edge. This is a minor prompt-faithfulness issue. The piece is coherent throughout, with no continuity errors. Character actions are consistently believable. The transformation from strangers to tentative connection is earned through accumulated small moments rather than dramatic gestures, which is exactly right for this kind of story. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 17 Pacing: 14 World Building: 13 Compelling Plot: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Unearned Transformations: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 14
Avg Final Item Score: 77.6/100
+ Space Western — Cargo Unknown (Chapter Avg: 80.6 | Final: 80.1)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Space Western Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: Captain, something's... moving in that crate we picked up.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Space Western Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: Captain, something&#x27;s... moving in that crate we picked up. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming **Genre Tone Ideas:** - Gritty, Firefly-esque space western with morally grey characters - Lighthearted and adventurous, like a pulp sci-fi western - Dark and tense, leaning horror — the thing in the crate is dangerous - Comedic misadventures of a crew that bites off more than they can chew - Melancholic and philosophical — the thing in the crate changes everyone **Character Ideas:** - Captain "Mae" Calloway — a tough, debt-ridden woman running a freight hauler on the fringes of colonized space. Ex-military, disillusioned, pragmatic - Decker — the nervous young cargo hand who finds the crate moving, eager to prove himself - Rook — the ship's engineer, cynical, seen too much, drinks too much - Doc Yuen — ship's medic, curious, the moral conscience of the crew - Opal — the ship's AI, dry humor, knows more than she lets on - The "something" in the crate — could be: a child, an alien creature, a fugitive, a dangerous bioweapon, a mythological creature from frontier legend, a genetically engineered animal, an android, a baby alien that imprints on the crew **What's in the Crate — Ideas:** - A child survivor of a massacre, hidden by a dying parent - An alien creature, small and seemingly harmless, but hunted by powerful forces - A political prisoner/refugee being smuggled by shadowy forces - A creature that is the last of its kind — sacred to indigenous space-frontier peoples - A shapeshifter or mimic creature - A government bioweapon that is sentient - A young dragon-like alien that imprints on the captain - A wounded outlaw who paid someone to smuggle him - An ancient alien artifact that is alive - Twin children, orphans of a destroyed colony **Plot Directions:** - The crew discovers what's inside, debates what to do, then gets hunted by whoever sent the crate - The creature/person bonds with the crew and they have to protect it - The thing escapes and the crew has to catch it while also being pursued - A moral dilemma: turn it in for money they desperately need, or do the right thing - The crate was meant as a weapon delivered somewhere — the crew stumbles into a conspiracy - The "something" turns out to be dangerous and the story becomes survival horror - The something is innocent and the real monster is the people chasing it **Western Parallels:** - Smuggling across frontier territories = running contraband across state lines - Corporate/government enforcers = bounty hunters/marshals - Lawless outer planets = frontier towns - The crew as outlaws with a code - A showdown/standoff at a remote frontier station - Themes of found family, loyalty, freedom vs. security **Story Beat Ideas:** - Ch1: Normal job, crate picked up, Decker hears the sound - Ch2: Crew investigates, discovers what's inside — big reveal - Ch3: Debate what to do — character dynamics explored - Ch4: Someone contacts them — the people who want it back (antagonist introduced) - Ch5: Escape/chase sequence, the thing proves itself (helpful, dangerous, endearing) - Ch6: Forced stop at a frontier station, tension, close call - Ch7: Confrontation — maybe a crew member's loyalty wavers - Ch8: Resolution — do they keep it, deliver it, set it free? **Possible Themes:** - What does freedom cost? - Found family among outcasts - Complicity — if you carry the cargo, are you responsible for it? - The powerful preying on the small and vulnerable - Humanity found in unexpected places **Antagonist Ideas:** - A corporate syndicate that created the creature - A cold government marshal who is "just following orders" - A bounty hunter who is eerily charming - The original client who hired the crew — betrayal angle - Multiple factions wanting the same thing for different reasons --- # Reflection The strongest version of this story lives at the intersection of the space western aesthetic and a found family emotional core — that's the genre's beating heart, and what makes something like Firefly resonate so deeply. The horror angle is tempting but risks losing the warmth that makes this kind of story memorable. A pure horror direction also fights against the western tone, which tends toward grit and tension rather than dread. The most compelling "something in the crate" is probably a creature — not a human — because it raises the stakes in an interesting way. A human fugitive is familiar. A sentient or semi-sentient alien creature that is small, vulnerable, and hunted gives the crew something to project onto, argue over, and ultimately bond with. It also lets the author play with frontier mythology, which fits the western aesthetic beautifully. Making it the last of its kind raises the moral weight considerably. Captain Mae Calloway feels like the right protagonist — a woman with debts and a code, who has learned not to care too much as a survival mechanism. The creature will be the thing that breaks that armor open. Decker as the viewpoint "everyman" who finds the crate is useful for the opening, but Mae should carry the story. The antagonist being a corporate enforcer — polished, legal, and utterly ruthless — works well against the scrappy crew. It creates a class/power dynamic that echoes classic westerns (the railroad baron vs. the homesteaders) without being on the nose. The wavering loyalty beat in chapter 7 is important — probably Rook, who is pragmatic enough to consider the payout. This creates internal conflict to mirror the external chase. The ending should be bittersweet — the western tradition doesn't always give you clean victories. Perhaps the creature is set free on a wild, uncharted planet, and the crew rides off poorer but more whole. That feels true to the genre and emotionally satisfying without being saccharine. Pacing across 8 chapters: the reveal should come early (Chapter 2), so the story isn't about *what* it is but about *what you do about it.* That's the more interesting question.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention **The Core Story:** Captain Mae Calloway and her small crew of misfits pick up a cargo crate on a routine haul across the frontier. Inside they find a creature — small, luminescent, and clearly the last of its kind — being transported by a powerful biotech corporation for reasons that become increasingly sinister. The story is about what the crew chooses to do with it, and what that choice costs them. **Plot Choices:** The reveal comes in Chapter 2 — the mystery of *what* is in the crate is secondary to the question of *what you do about it.* The antagonist is a corporate enforcer named Harlan Voss, who operates with the full legal authority of the Meridian Biotech Corporation and is more dangerous for being polite about it. The crew will be pursued, cornered, and forced to make hard choices. One crew member (Rook) will waver under financial pressure. The ending will be bittersweet — the creature is freed on an uncharted world, the crew survives but pays a price, and Mae is left a little more human than she started. **Characters:** - **Captain Mae Calloway** — protagonist, ex-military, emotionally armored, pragmatic to a fault. The creature gets under her skin in ways she can't explain and won't admit. - **Decker** — young cargo hand, the audience surrogate, instinctively kind, the one who first wants to protect it. - **Rook** — engineer, older, cynical, financially desperate. His wavering loyalty creates the internal crisis of act three. - **Doc Yuen** — medic, curious and methodical, provides the crew with crucial information about what the creature is and why Meridian wants it. - **Opal** — the ship's AI, dry and understated, occasional dark humor, occasional unsettling insight. - **The Creature** — small enough to hold, faintly luminescent, communicates through resonant pulses of light and sound. Not a pet, not a person — something in between that defies easy categorization. Named "Cinder" by Decker. - **Harlan Voss** — Meridian enforcer. Calm, well-dressed, legally unimpeachable. The most dangerous kind of villain: one with paperwork. **Tone:** Gritty but warm at its core — the Firefly register. Tense when it needs to be, with room for dry humor and quiet character moments. The western elements (frontier planets, dust and rock, long silences, standoffs) are worn naturally into the fabric of the world rather than announced. The horror of the situation comes from bureaucratic evil, not from the creature itself. **Voice & Style:** Third-person limited, following Mae's perspective throughout. Prose should be lean and concrete — working-class diction that reflects Mae's character. Short sentences under pressure. More breath in the quieter scenes. Dialogue carries character weight. Avoid purple prose, avoid over-explaining the world. The setting should be felt in texture and detail, not in exposition dumps. The western and sci-fi elements should feel fully fused — no seams. **Themes:** - Complicity and conscience: carrying the cargo makes you responsible for it - Found family forged under pressure - The powerful laundering cruelty through legality - What it means to do the right thing when it costs you something real **What to Avoid:** - The creature becoming a simple MacGuffin — it must feel genuinely alive and specific - Rook's betrayal being unforgivable or cartoonish — it should be understandable - A clean, triumphant ending — the victory should be earned and come with a scar - Over-explaining the world's lore or the creature's biology - Mae's emotional arc being too fast or too neat --- # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 — "The Pickup" (~1000 words)** Mae and her crew dock at Sutter's Depot, a rust-bucket waystation on the edge of surveyed space, to collect a routine freight job: three crates of mining equipment bound for the Callisto Reach. The pay is modest but the crew needs it — Rook mentions the engine coolant situation, Opal flags three overdue maintenance cycles. The job is brokered by a nervous middleman who insists on no scanning of the cargo, which Mae notes but accepts — it's not her first no-questions haul. The crates are loaded. They leave. As they break atmo, Decker is running inventory in the hold when he comes up to the bridge, pale-faced, and delivers the line: *"Captain, something's... moving in that crate we picked up."* Mae's response: she finishes her coffee first. **Chapter 2 — "Cinder" (~1000 words)** Mae, Decker, and Rook open the crate in the hold. Inside, packed among the legitimate mining equipment like an afterthought, is a creature — roughly the size of a cat, vaguely reptilian, with a faintly luminescent hide that pulses when frightened. It's alive, it's clearly intelligent in some animal-adjacent way, and it's terrified. Doc Yuen is called down. She identifies it as something she's only read about in classified ecological surveys — a Vaeli, from the deep canyon systems of an uncharted moon. The last confirmed population was catalogued by Meridian Biotech six months ago. It hadn't been made public. Decker names it Cinder, against Mae's explicit instruction not to name it. The crew debate: dump it at the next port, deliver it with the crate, or figure out what's actually going on. Mae says they finish the job. Decker looks at Cinder. Cinder pulses soft amber — something that, Doc notes quietly, Vaeli do when they feel safe. **Chapter 3 — "What the Manifest Says" (~1000 words)** Doc Yuen digs into the cargo manifest and cross-references her own medical databases overnight. At breakfast, she lays it out: Meridian Biotech has been quietly collecting Vaeli for their biochemical research — the creatures produce a compound in their nervous tissue that is extraordinarily valuable for neural interface technology. The process of extracting it is not survivable. Cinder is almost certainly the last living specimen. The crew sits with this. Mae asks what the creature is worth alive on the black market. Doc tells her. So does Rook — he's already looked it up. Mae catches his eye. Meanwhile, Cinder has migrated from the hold to the mess, is eating their food stores, and has burned a small perfect circle into Decker's bunk with its tail. Character work and tension — Mae's pragmatism versus the crew's growing attachment. Mae goes to the cockpit and has a quiet conversation with Opal, who says: *"You've already decided, Captain. You're just waiting to be angry about it."* **Chapter 4 — "Meridian Calling" (~1000 words)** A transmission from Harlan Voss, Meridian's Senior Acquisitions Officer. He's polite. He's aware of exactly which ship picked up that crate. He explains there's been a "cataloguing error" in their shipment, and he'd like to arrange a recovery. He offers them three times their contract fee to deliver the full cargo — no questions, no consequences. Mae asks what's in the crate. He says industrial survey equipment. She says that's what the manifest says too. She tells him they'll be in touch and cuts the feed. The crew reconvenes. Rook makes the case that the money would fix everything — the ship, the debts, six months of breathing room. He's not wrong. Decker is horrified. Doc is quiet. Mae gives no answer. That night, Decker sits in the hold with Cinder, who pulses soft colors in the dark. He talks to it like it understands. Maybe it does. **Chapter 5 — "Hard Burn" (~1000 words)** Mae plots a course deviation — they're not going to the Callisto Reach. She tells the crew they're going to find somewhere to put Cinder down safely before they finish the job. Rook objects loudly. Mae makes it clear this is not a democracy. Before they can execute, Opal detects a Meridian company ship dropping out of transit behind them — Voss didn't wait for a callback. The chase begins. Rook, furious but loyal to the ship, pushes the engines hard. The tension of the chase is cut through with Cinder's behavior — the creature is agitated, pulsing red, moving around the ship. Then it does something unexpected: it seems to be responding to the ship's systems, pulsing in sync with Opal's navigation displays. Opal notes, with her usual dryness, that the creature appears to have an electromagnetic sensitivity — it's essentially reading the ship. They lose Voss in the debris field of an old mining belt.
