"Stand." > She waits. > "Rowan berries are early this year," he says, and she knows from his tone that he's not changing the subject — he's adding it to the same document. *Internal (from Maren's POV, watching Rook at the fence before dawn):* > He had the posture of someone reading — present and still and pulling meaning out of a thing that wasn't giving it up easily. She'd watched birders once, on a coastal path before all of this, and they'd all had this quality: the patience of people who understood that the world would show you what it was doing if you just stayed quiet long enough. She wondered if that was comforting, knowing that. She suspected that right now, for him, it was not. *Rook, at the table at 4am, after a long silence, to no one in particular:* > "I found a wood thrush last week. Sitting in the same tree six days running. Just — sitting there, facing south." He has a mug of tea he hasn't drunk. "That's the wrong direction. For this time of year." > Nobody knows what to say. > "Just thought I'd mention it," he says, and looks out the window.

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 1 — *Mise en Place* The house had been picked clean by someone more optimistic than thorough. They'd taken the obvious things — tins, dry goods, anything in packaging — and left behind the evidence of their thinking: cupboard doors hanging open, a trail of small decisions through every room. Maren worked through the kitchen in the order she always worked through kitchens, which was the order most likely to find what other people had missed, and what she found was a broken torch, three birthday candles, a ceramic rooster that someone had decided wasn't worth carrying, and one can of Campbell's tomato soup with a puncture wound in its side that had let the air in probably fourteen months ago. She held it up. The can was swollen slightly at one end in a way that meant it had made up its mind about the situation. She set it on the counter and kept looking. From the front room, nothing. From Jonah, also nothing, which meant he'd already done the front room and found the same. He'd been through the whole house before she'd finished the kitchen, the way he always did — quick, systematic, good instincts — and now she could hear the specific silence of him sitting somewhere with his sheet music book, which was what he did when there was nothing left to do and he didn't want to say so directly. The soup can was still on the counter when she came back to it. She picked it up again, not because she'd changed her mind about it but because she couldn't quite make herself put it in the bag with the birthday candles and call that a day. She pressed the swollen end with her thumb. It gave slightly, which it shouldn't. She put it down. *Right,* she thought, and did not allow herself to think anything further in that direction. Jonah appeared in the kitchen doorway with the sheet music book held open at a page she recognized — he'd been on the same page for three days, the one about treble clefs, which had apparently become a personal matter between him and the book. "There's a back room I didn't check," he said. "But something went through the back door from outside, so." "How long ago?" He considered this with the particular expression that meant he was being precise rather than cautious. "Months. The wood's dried out where it split." He tilted his head toward the back of the house. "The pattern's wrong for something looking for food. It was just — moving through." Maren looked at the ceiling, did the brief calculation. "We'll check it." The back room had been a child's bedroom once. The furniture was still there because furniture was heavy and optimism had its limits. Whatever had come through the back door had come through with enough force to take the lower hinge off and hadn't come in here — or had come in and gone back out, which was worse to think about so she noted it and moved on. Nothing useful. Under the bed, a single shoe, which she looked at for a moment before leaving it. Outside the window, the yard was going to scrub. A trampoline frame with no mat, grown through with something that had found the rusted legs useful for climbing. The light was getting toward late afternoon, going orange and thickening in the way that meant they needed to decide soon. She was aware of being hungry in the distant, background way that had become her body's default setting. Not acute. Just the constant low-frequency signal that said *this is the situation* and then said nothing further because there was nothing further to say. Jonah was in the doorway again. "There was smoke," he said. "When we came in. South." "I know." "We could follow it tonight. Make a few kilometers before dark." "Smoke means people," Maren said. "Yes." "People are complicated." "Also yes." He looked at the trampoline frame in the yard with the mild interest he brought to most things. "So is day four." She didn't answer that, because it didn't need answering. He wasn't wrong and she wasn't arguing. She was just — she was looking at the yard, at the trampoline frame, at the place where the late light was doing something almost courteous to the ruin of the garden. There had been sunflowers here once, she could tell from the skeletal remains of the canes, a whole row of them along the back fence. Someone had planted them on purpose, staked them, cared about them. The canes were grey now and fallen into each other like a bad hand of cards. *Okay,* she thought, and that was that. She was picking up her bag when she saw it at the edge of the yard, half behind the fence post where the garden ended and the scrub began. She went still without deciding to. It had been human, once, or it had been built on human architecture — the height was right, and somewhere inside the fungal structure she could see the shape of a shoulder. The growth ran up one side of its neck and opened across the left part of its face like a hand pressed against a window. In the late light it was faintly, coldly luminescent where the fungal bodies were thickest. It was looking at the house. It was standing perfectly still in the specific way that the more advanced ones stood, which was too still, without the small constant adjustments that bodies made unconsciously just to stay upright. It was, in the failing light, briefly and horribly beautiful. Maren said nothing. She named it. *Louise,* she thought, for no reason she could account for. *Okay, Louise.* Then she looked at Jonah and found he was already looking at her, already reading whatever was on her face, already picking up his bag. They left through the front.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong opening chapter that demonstrates considerable craft. Let me work through its qualities systematically. **Voice and Prose Quality** The prose is controlled and precise in the way the plan demands. The narration stays close to Maren's perspective without becoming claustrophobic, and the voice is genuinely dry without being performatively so. Sentences vary well — "She held it up. The can was swollen slightly at one end in a way that meant it had made up its mind about the situation" is a good example of the intended register: deadpan, specific, slightly funny without winking. The soup can detail is excellent — the swollen end, the thumb test, the decision not to decide — this is showing character through action rather than telling. The "bad hand of cards" simile for the fallen sunflower canes is earned and specific. The "birthday candles" and "ceramic rooster" as evidence of someone else's triage thinking is a smart way to establish the world through absence. **Character Consistency** Maren is rendered faithfully to her profile. The naming of Louise at the end is handled with exactly the right restraint — no explanation, no sentiment, just the fact of it. Her deflection from the hunger ("the constant low-frequency signal that said this is the situation and then said nothing further") is precise and in-character. Her relationship with Jonah is established through texture rather than exposition — the silence of him with the sheet music book, her reading his face at the end. Jonah is well-rendered in limited space. His sheet music book, his systematic efficiency, his directness ("So is day four"), his observation about the door pattern — all consistent with the profile. The detail about him being on the same treble clef page for three days is a nice specific touch. **Chapter Plan Adherence** The chapter follows its plan closely. The stripped house, the punctured can, Jonah with the sheet music book, the wrong break-in pattern, the smoke discussion, the luminescent mutant at the yard's edge — all present. The plan called for Maren to name Louise silently without telling Jonah, and this is executed correctly. The chapter ends as planned. **Weaknesses** The chapter is perhaps slightly too clean. The plan mentions "dark humor" as a primary texture, and while the humor is present (the soup can, the ceramic rooster, "people are complicated / also yes"), it's fairly restrained. This isn't necessarily a flaw — the chapter is establishing tone rather than delivering on all registers at once — but the humor could be slightly more present. The dialogue is minimal but functional. "People are complicated / So is day four" is the best exchange and it works. There's nothing weak here, but also nothing that particularly distinguishes itself. The child's bedroom scene is handled efficiently but the single shoe detail, while appropriate, is a fairly familiar post-apocalyptic image. It doesn't quite reach the specificity the rest of the chapter achieves. The Louise moment at the end is the chapter's strongest beat and is handled with appropriate restraint. The "briefly and horribly beautiful" phrasing is the closest the chapter comes to purple prose — it's a slight tell rather than show, naming the effect rather than trusting the image to produce it. The image itself (the fungal growth like a hand pressed against a window, the too-still posture) is strong enough that the editorial "briefly and horribly beautiful" is slightly redundant. **Overall Assessment** This is a confident, well-executed opening chapter. It establishes world, character, and tone without over-explaining any of them. The prose is controlled and specific. The horror is present but not foregrounded. The cozy-horror simultaneity the plan calls for is achieved in miniature — the late light doing something "almost courteous" to the ruin, the luminescent mutant that is beautiful and wrong at once. The chapter does what it needs to do and doesn't try to do more. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 2 — *The Kill* They made three kilometers south before the light went and stopped at a petrol station that Maren had been watching from the road for long enough to be reasonably sure about. The forecourt was open, which she liked. The shop had been gone through but the service bay still had its roller door and the roller door still worked, which she liked more. They got it down to knee height and stopped it there — low enough to see out, high enough to slow something down. Jonah found a corner and had his sheet music book out before she'd finished checking the perimeter of the bay. She didn't say anything about this. He'd made his assessment of the space when they came in; his assessment and hers had evidently agreed. His way of saying *we're fine for now* was to do other things. She sat with her back against the wall and ate half of the three biscuits she'd been carrying for two days and gave him the other half and he ate them in the deliberate way he ate everything, like the chewing was part of something he was thinking about. Outside, the dark came down. She listened to it arrive. She was most of the way into sleep when it came under the roller door. She heard it before she saw it — the sound it made was wrong in the particular way the sounds they made were always wrong, which was not that they were loud but that they were patterned incorrectly, like something doing an impression of movement rather than moving. She was on her feet with the hatchet in her hand before she'd consciously decided anything, which was how the body worked now, how she'd trained it to work, and across the bay she could see Jonah already standing with the long knife in a low grip. The thing came under the roller door sideways, which meant it understood the obstacle, which meant it was one of the worse kind. In the ambient dark she couldn't see detail — just mass and the faint luminescence and the wrongness of how it moved, too smooth and too deliberate, navigating the space with the confidence of something that could afford to be patient. She didn't name it yet. She named them when she was going to kill them, not before. It came for her, not Jonah, which was random or wasn't random and either way it didn't matter. She went left, made it follow her, gave Jonah the angle he needed, and when he moved she moved and it was over in less time than it took to understand it was happening. It was not clean. These things were never clean. She'd stopped expecting clean. Afterward they stood in the dark and breathed. *Derek,* she thought, which was not the right name for it and was also the only name that had come, and that was all. She was wiping the hatchet on a rag when Jonah said: "We should eat it." She stopped wiping. "I know," he said. "You don't know what I was going to say." "You weren't going to say anything yet. But I know what you'll think." He was still holding the long knife and he looked at it and then crouched down and started cleaning it with the methodical care he brought to the knife every time, which she'd always found steadying to watch and found less steadying right now. "The spore affects the nervous system. The brain. It doesn't touch the muscle tissue — I've read enough about it, early on, people wrote about it before the networks went." He paused. "It's just meat, Maren." "You've thought about this before." "Yes." "For how long." He looked up at her. In the dark she couldn't fully read his expression but she knew the shape of it — the careful, neutral look that meant he was deciding how precise to be. "Month two," he said. "About." Month two. They had been together for thirteen months. She heard herself say: "So you've just been waiting. For me to be desperate enough." "Yes." "And if I'd never gotten desperate enough?" He thought about it with the quality of attention he gave the sheet music book, the trying-to-hear-something quality. "I think you were always going to," he said. She picked up the hatchet. She was aware she was holding the hatchet while having this conversation and decided that was fine, the hatchet was fine, she was just holding it. The thing he'd said — *I've been waiting* — sat in her chest in a way she didn't have time for right now. That he'd been watching her. Taking the measure of her limits. Deciding when she'd be ready, as if readiness was something that could be calculated for someone else. She didn't like it. She didn't say she didn't like it. She looked at what was on the floor. "I'll do it," she said. "You get a fire." She did the butchering by feel and by the knowledge her hands had, because if she thought about it explicitly she would have to have a reaction and the reaction would not be useful. She focused on the anatomical logic of it, the same logic that made it possible to break down a lamb shoulder or joint a chicken — find the structure, follow the structure, let the structure tell you where to go. The fungal growth was external, mostly. What was underneath was muscle and connective tissue arranged in the way that these things were arranged. She focused on this. The fire Jonah built was small and correct, sheltered from the gap under the roller door. When she brought the meat to it the smell hit her before she expected it to — not the smell she'd braced for but something else entirely, something that made the back of her throat respond before she could stop it. Rich and dark, a little smoky from the fat hitting the fire, genuinely appetizing in the way that good meat over a real fire was genuinely appetizing, which was to say completely and without apology. Her mouth watered. She noted this and said nothing. They cooked it through. She was thorough about this. They ate sitting against the wall with the roller door at knee height and the dark outside doing nothing, just being dark. The food was good. She didn't say this. She thought it with something that was close to anger and ate her portion and then most of what she'd set aside as the remainder and when she noticed she was doing that she finished it deliberately rather than stop. Jonah ate slowly, the way he always ate. He didn't say anything either. The fire went down. She didn't sleep, after. She sat and took inventory of her body every twenty minutes or so, looking for the wrongness that didn't come, and outside the dark did nothing, and eventually the dark became the grey that came before morning.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter executes its plan with considerable skill and restraint. The writing demonstrates a clear, confident voice that belongs to Maren without ever becoming self-congratulatory about it. Let me work through the key elements. **Voice and Prose Quality** The prose is controlled and precise in the way the plan demands. Sentences vary effectively — the short declaratives land without feeling like affectations, and the longer sentences genuinely run the way tired thoughts run. "The sound it made was wrong in the particular way the sounds they made were always wrong, which was not that they were loud but that they were patterned incorrectly, like something doing an impression of movement rather than moving" — this is genuinely good. It earns its length. The comparison to "something doing an impression of movement" is specific and strange in the right way. The naming habit — "Derek" — is handled with exactly the right touch. It's introduced, it's slightly wrong (she acknowledges this), and it's dropped without ceremony. No over-explanation. **The Central Scene** The conversation after the kill is the chapter's heart, and it works. Jonah's revelation that he's been waiting since month two is genuinely unsettling in the way the plan intends — not because he's sinister but because of the patience it implies, the private calculation. The dialogue is spare and does real work. "I think you were always going to" is a good line that reveals character without announcing itself as a character-revealing line. The hatchet detail — Maren noting she's holding it during the conversation and deciding that's fine — is exactly the kind of specific, slightly dark humor the plan calls for. It doesn't wink. It just is. **The Butchering and Cooking** The decision to have Maren butcher by feel, focusing on "anatomical logic," is smart. It shows her coping mechanism (competence as avoidance) without stating it. The smell betrayal — "something that made the back of her throat respond before she could stop it" — is well-handled. The mouth-watering noted and said nothing is good. The ending — eating the remainder deliberately rather than stopping, the inventory-taking through the night — is appropriately unresolved. The chapter ends mid-experience, as planned, without verdict. **Weaknesses** The fight scene is competent but slightly generic. "She went left, made it follow her, gave Jonah the angle he needed, and when he moved she moved and it was over in less time than it took to understand it was happening" — this is efficient but doesn't have the specificity the prose achieves elsewhere. The mutant itself is described with some care ("too smooth and too deliberate, navigating the space with the confidence of something that could afford to be patient") but the description is slightly abstract where it could be more concrete. "The dark outside doing nothing, just being dark" at the end is a minor stumble — it's trying for something and lands slightly flat, a little too self-consciously minimal. The plan called for the mutant to be described "with the full attention the prose can give it" — the chapter doesn't quite deliver this. The description is adequate but not the sustained, careful attention promised. The face "which is still a face, or the arrangement of features that used to be one" from the plan doesn't appear; the mutant remains somewhat abstract. **Dialogue** The dialogue is genuinely strong. It's spare, it reveals character, it doesn't carry thematic weight efficiently and neatly — it's messy in the right ways. Jonah's "I think you were always going to" is the best line. The exchange about waiting is uncomfortable in the productive way the plan intends. **Consistency with Plan and Profile** The chapter follows its plan closely. The naming, the butchering by reflex, the smell betrayal, the eating without verdict, the night inventory — all present. Maren's voice is consistent with her profile: dry, precise, deflecting through competence. Jonah's private reasoning revealed only partially, his settled quality, his directness — all consistent. The secondary undercurrent (Jonah's patience, what else he might be privately reasoning toward) is present but not overplayed. **Overall** This is accomplished work. It's not a masterpiece — the fight scene is underwritten relative to the rest, the mutant description doesn't fully deliver on the plan's promise, and the final line is slightly weak. But the prose is controlled, the characters are real, the central scene earns its discomfort, and the cozy/horror simultaneity is genuinely achieved in the eating scene. The chapter does what it sets out to do with skill and without the common failure modes (over-explanation, thematic tidiness, false resolution). [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 3 — *Rest* She woke twice in the night from a sleep she hadn't meant to fall into. The first time she woke she lay still and ran the inventory: ceiling, exit, Jonah's breathing — there, normal, the particular rhythm of him — and then her body, systematically, looking for the thing that would announce itself as wrong. Nothing did. She noted this and lay there for a while and then slept again without meaning to. The second time she woke it was from a dream she couldn't hold onto, something about the restaurant, the particular sound the pass made when Theo slammed tickets down, and she was awake and the sound resolved into Jonah shifting position across the bay. She lay still again. Did the inventory again. Her body reported back the same as before, which was: present, functional, adequately fed. Adequately fed. She sat up. Jonah was already awake, or had been awake, or possibly hadn't slept in the way he sometimes didn't sleep — she could never quite tell. He was at the roller door looking out at the forecourt in the early grey light with the sheet music book closed on his knee, which meant he'd been watching and thinking rather than reading, which he did when there was something to watch. "Anything?" she said. "No." He paused. "Smoke's still there. South-southwest, I think it moved a little overnight. Wind." She got up and looked. She couldn't see smoke — too much grey in the sky and too little contrast — but she could smell something at the edge of things when the air shifted. Woodsmoke, the sustained kind. Not a camp fire. "That's been going all night," she said. "That's what I thought." She pulled the roller door up and stood in the forecourt and breathed the morning air until she had it properly. There. South-southwest, the way he'd said. Whatever was burning had been burning long enough to be habitual, which meant either very stupid people or very established people, and very stupid people didn't maintain fires through the night. She stood there for longer than was necessary. The morning was cold and flat and the forecourt's cracked concrete was growing something determined through every joint. She looked at it and thought about the day ahead in the practical sense — how far south, what terrain, what they'd be walking toward — and her body continued to report: functional, adequately fed, not dying. She turned around. Jonah was in the roller door with his bag already on. "People are complicated," she said. "You said that." "I'm saying it again." "Do you want to not go?" She looked south. She thought about day four, which Jonah had mentioned last night and which she hadn't argued with because there was nothing to argue with. She thought about the inventory she'd been running all night, the way her body had responded to her questions with a steadiness it hadn't had in three days, the simple animal fact of having eaten. She thought about the smoke, which had been going all night, which meant someone had put fuel on it in the dark. Someone was tending something. "I want to be careful," she said. "Let's go." They found the valley two hours later, coming over a rise she hadn't seen coming — the land had been flat and then it wasn't, and then below them, set back from a stream that caught the morning light, was a farm. A working farm, or the closest thing to it: the farmhouse solid and clearly occupied, outbuildings that had been repaired with recent timber, a garden along the south face that was real and tended with winter brassicas still standing in rows. A line of washing. Figures moving between the house and the outbuildings with the purposeful economy of people who had somewhere to be. Maren stopped. Her body did something she hadn't given it permission to do, which was to simply orient toward it, the way a plant did with light, some mechanism below decision. She stood at the top of the rise and felt it happen and didn't move. Jonah stood beside her and was quiet, which was his version of the same thing. They watched for a long time. Nobody in the valley looked up. The smoke came from the farmhouse chimney and from what looked like a smaller outbuilding, two separate fires, and the smell of it reached them on the wind carrying something underneath the woodsmoke — something cooking, something that had been cooking long enough to reduce and concentrate, something that made Maren's adequately-fed stomach register interest in a way she found somewhat humiliating. "How do you want to do this?" Jonah said. "Slowly," she said. "In the open. Hands visible." They came down the slope in the open, taking the most visible line, not hurrying. A figure near the outbuilding stopped and watched them come. Another figure appeared at the farmhouse door and stood there. By the time they reached the flat ground before the fence a third person had materialized and was walking toward them from the gate — a man, thirties, moving with the quiet lateral awareness of someone who spent a lot of time outside and knew how to cross ground without announcing himself. He stopped at the gate and looked at them with a calm, thorough attention that took in their bags, their hands, their faces, the state of them. "Mountain ash," he said, and then, seeing their expressions: "Over your left shoulder, on the rise. Most people think they're rowans. The berry clusters are different if you know what to look for." He unlatched the gate. "I'm Rook. You look like you've been walking." "Two hours this morning," Jonah said. "Longer than that," Rook said, looking at them with the same careful attention, and stood aside to let them through. The farmhouse was warm. This was the first thing — the warmth of it, real warmth from real fires, hitting her in the face when the door opened and doing something to her chest that she didn't examine because she was busy taking in the room. A kitchen, large, with a long table and mismatched chairs and a chalkboard on the wall with a weekly menu written in careful cursive. A row of ceramic animals along the windowsill arranged with a precision that suggested someone cared very much about the arrangement. The smell she'd caught on the rise was here in full, coming from a pot on the stove, and it was extraordinary and she stood in the doorway and breathed it for a moment before she remembered herself. A woman appeared from around the stove who was short and grey-haired and holding a dish towel and said: "Now then. Sit down." They sat down. Bette — she gave her name while she moved, not as an introduction exactly, more as information she was providing while doing other things — put bowls in front of them before the sitting down had fully resolved, and then bread which was real bread, dense and slightly uneven but real, and she was already talking about the garden, something about the brassicas and the likely frost timeline, and Maren registered this at a distance because the food was in front of her and her body had already made several decisions. She ate fast at first and then slowed down, because the food was genuinely good and she was not yet so diminished that she couldn't register this, and slowing down was a way of — she didn't finish the thought. She ate. The broth was rich and dark, the vegetables properly cooked, something in it she couldn't immediately place, a depth to it that she couldn't account for from vegetables alone. She ate most of a second bowl before she noticed she was eating a second bowl. At some point she became aware that Bette had stopped talking about the brassicas and the kitchen had gone fairly quiet and Jonah was looking at her with a neutral expression that she knew meant he was waiting for her to do something. She looked at the bowl. She looked at Bette, who was at the stove with her back partly turned, stirring something, entirely unperformed in her lack of attention. Maren thought: *I know what's in this.* She thought: *I'm not going to ask.* The thought arrived without drama and settled. She looked at the bowl and at the bread in her hand and at the second bowl she had eaten most of without deciding to, and she understood that she had already made the decision, back at the petrol station or maybe at the rise above the valley or maybe on the third day without food in a kitchen with a ruined can of soup, and the decision was made and the bowl was empty. She set down her spoon. Bette turned from the stove and met her eyes with a gaze that was steady and entirely ordinary, the gaze of someone who had said everything she was going to say and was now waiting, without pressure, to see what came next. Maren looked back at her. "Thank you," Maren said. "For the food." "You're welcome," Bette said, and turned back to the stove.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter executes its plan with considerable skill and restraint. The writing plan called for "arrival, warmth, the physical relief of eating" as primary texture, with "the choice not to ask, which is its own ethical act" as secondary undercurrent, and the chapter delivers both with precision. The opening inventory sequence — ceiling, exit, Jonah's breathing — is economical and characterizes Maren efficiently without over-explaining. The repetition of "adequately fed" as a paragraph break, followed by "She sat up," is a small but effective piece of craft: the horror is inside the comfort, exactly as the plan intended. The prose doesn't announce this; it trusts the reader. Rook's introduction is handled well. His mountain ash/rowan non-sequitur lands as character rather than quirk-signaling — it's genuinely him, and the line "I'm Rook. You look like you've been walking" has the right dry quality. The character profiles are honored throughout: Bette's "Now then. Sit down" and her narrating while cooking, Jonah's quiet patience, Rook's careful observational attention. The central ethical moment — Maren's decision not to ask — is the chapter's strongest passage. "She thought: I know what's in this. She thought: I'm not going to ask." The decision arriving "without drama and settling" is exactly right. The plan specified this should not be dramatized, and it isn't. The retrospective accounting of when the decision was made ("back at the petrol station or maybe at the rise above the valley or maybe on the third day without food") is genuinely good — it captures how ethical capitulations work, how they're never quite located in a single moment. The final exchange with Bette is earned. "Thank you for the food" / "You're welcome" — the plainness of it, the mutual understanding without articulation, is the right ending for this chapter. Bette's "steady and entirely ordinary" gaze is shown rather than explained. Some minor weaknesses: The plant-orienting-toward-light simile ("the way a plant did with light, some mechanism below decision") is the chapter's one moment of slightly strained metaphor — it's not terrible, but it's the most visible piece of craft in a chapter that otherwise wears its craft lightly, and it draws attention to itself. The description of the farmhouse interior (ceramic animals, chalkboard with weekly menu) is handled efficiently and doesn't overstay its welcome, though the chalkboard with "careful cursive" is a minor cozy-signifier that edges toward the type of detail the plan said to avoid. The chapter is coherent, well-paced, and consistent with all character profiles. Jonah's minimal dialogue is exactly right for him. Bette's warmth-with-firmness is present without being overdone. The horror is genuinely inside the comfort rather than adjacent to it. The dialogue is sparse but functional — no line carries more thematic weight than it should, and the exchanges feel like actual speech rather than efficient information delivery. "People are complicated" / "You said that" / "I'm saying it again" is a small, good moment that characterizes both of them without effort. The chapter follows its plan closely and faithfully. The writing prompt's dark comic premise is honored in the specific way the plan described — Maren knows what she's eating, doesn't ask, and the horror is in the comfort rather than opposed to it. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 4 — *Salt* They stayed the night because it was offered and because the alternative was the dark. They stayed the second night because there was a frost and because Bette put extra blankets at the foot of their beds without being asked and because in the morning there was breakfast. By the third day Maren had stopped counting the nights and this was either a good sign or a bad sign and she couldn't decide which so she went to work in the kitchen and didn't think about it. The settlement had eleven people, and they accumulated for Maren the way places accumulated — not all at once but in layers, a new detail every few hours until the place became itself rather than a general idea of itself. The couple who argued about the storage room were called Gil and Petra and the argument had been running, as best anyone could tell, since August, which meant it had outlasted several other things that people had assumed were more durable. The argument was about whether the room should hold root vegetables, which needed cool and dark, or equipment, which needed to be accessible and dry — both reasonable positions, Gil and Petra maintained theirs with the energy of people who had found, in disagreement, a way of touching each other constantly, and everyone in the settlement had identified this and left them to it. The ceramic animals on the kitchen windowsill belonged to a quiet woman named Soo who had arranged them in an order that Maren could not identify as random but also couldn't decode as intentional. She had touched one of them, slightly, to see the view from the window better, and Soo had come into the kitchen twenty minutes later and corrected it without looking at her, with the precise, two-millimeter adjustment of someone who'd noticed immediately but waited until she could deal with it privately. Maren had not touched them again. There were others: a teenage boy called Fitz who had adopted Jonah with the immediate certainty of someone whose social world had been reduced to adults for long enough that another young person registered as an event; a man in his fifties named Bernard who had a cough he'd been managing since winter and who made up for being occasionally infirm by having fixed more things around the settlement than everyone else combined; an older couple, Mae and Desmond, who had arrived together from somewhere north and who sat close to each other at the table in the manner of people who had decided, early and finally, that proximity was a reasonable response to the world. Jonah moved through all of this as if he'd been here before. He was with Fitz within the first day — she could hear them outside, Jonah explaining something about camp setup with the focused specificity he brought to things he'd reasoned through, Fitz apparently arguing back, which meant Fitz was not simply adopting Jonah but engaging him, which was better. By the second day Jonah had his sheet music book out at the dinner table and was explaining to anyone who would listen the specific problem he was having with pitch recognition, and Mae had said she'd played piano, once, and couldn't remember if she could still, and Jonah had looked at her with the look that meant he would be following up on this. Maren watched him and felt something she couldn't immediately name, a warmth that had an edge on it like it might cut if she held it wrong. She worked in the kitchen because Bette put her to work and because she needed something to do with her hands. This was not discussed. Bette handed her a knife and a cutting board on the morning of the second day and told her what they were making and that was the whole of it. The kitchen was Bette's in a way that was completely clear and not aggressive — the way a professional kitchen was organized around the person who ran it, every tool in a known place, every process in a known order, and you learned the order or you were in the way. Maren learned the order. She was good at this, had always been good at this — coming into someone else's system and finding where she fit without requiring the system to adjust for her. By the second afternoon she knew where things lived and what Bette's preferences were and one other thing: that Bette consistently under-salted everything by a margin that was not catastrophic but was consistent and specific and which everyone in the settlement had developed a private workaround for. At dinner that night she watched Gil reach for the salt without pausing his conversation and Petra do the same from the other side and Rook, at the window-seat, salt his bowl with the focused attention he brought to most things before he'd taken a bite. Nobody said anything. Bette served and talked and was warm and wrong about the salt and everyone ate and was fed. Maren salted her bowl and thought: *twenty-two years teaching people to cook and she under-salts.* And then she thought: *I would die before I said anything.* And then she ate and the food was good. On the third day Rook came to dinner and sat in his seat by the window and ate and at a gap in the conversation said, without particular emphasis: "The south path's been interesting lately." He said it the way he said most things — as information he was providing rather than a point he was making — and the conversation moved on and he didn't elaborate and Maren noticed this and filed it in the part of her attention she kept for things that didn't fit. Later, after dinner, she saw him from the window of the room she'd been given. He was standing at the far edge of the field in the dark, quite still, facing south. He stood there for a long time. She couldn't tell if he was looking at something or listening for something or simply standing, doing the thing she did when she was working through a problem — except she reorganized surfaces and he apparently just stood in fields in the dark. She watched him for a few minutes and then got ready for bed and when she looked again he was gone. She lay in the bed, which was a real bed with a real mattress that was somewhat past its best and completely extraordinary, and looked at the ceiling and thought about the south path being interesting and thought about the way the settlement's people reached for the salt without needing to acknowledge why and thought about Jonah's face at dinner when Mae had said she'd played piano once. Her shoulders, she noticed, were not up around her ears. She noticed this the way you noticed something that had changed position — by its absence, by the shape of the space it left. She checked it like she checked everything: *is this real, is this reliable, what does it cost.* The answers were unclear. She looked at the ceiling for a while longer and then she slept, and this time she didn't wake up until morning.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 4 ("Salt") is a quiet, accumulative chapter that does exactly what the plan calls for: it builds the settlement into a real place through layered specificity rather than exposition, and it holds the cozy and the unsettling in simultaneous tension without forcing either to dominate. The prose is controlled and precise throughout. The opening paragraph's rhythm — "They stayed the night because it was offered and because the alternative was the dark. They stayed the second night because there was a frost..." — establishes Maren's voice immediately: dry, sequential, slightly self-aware about her own rationalizations. The repetition of "because" is deliberate and earns its keep; it mimics the way someone talks themselves into something without admitting they're doing it. This is character-consistent and formally intelligent. The settlement's inhabitants are introduced with economy and specificity. Gil and Petra's storage-room argument is rendered with genuine wit: "the energy of people who had found, in disagreement, a way of touching each other constantly" is a sharp, earned observation that tells you everything about their dynamic without sentimentality. Soo's ceramic animals and her silent two-millimeter correction is excellent — it's specific, slightly unsettling, and completely believable. These are not cozy signifiers; they are particular truths, as the plan demanded. The salt detail is the chapter's structural spine and it works beautifully. Bette's consistent under-salting, the settlement's collective silent workaround, Maren's internal response ("I would die before I said anything") — this is the chapter's best passage. It functions simultaneously as character revelation (Bette's flaw, the settlement's social texture, Maren's dry humor) and as a subtle thematic statement about what communities do with uncomfortable truths: they compensate quietly and never address it directly. The parallel to the spore-meat ethics is never stated, which is exactly right. Jonah's integration into the settlement is handled well — the detail about Mae and the piano, Jonah's look "that meant he would be following up on this," is perfectly in character. The observation about Maren watching him and feeling "a warmth that had an edge on it like it might cut if she held it wrong" is one of the chapter's few moments of more explicit metaphor, and it lands because it's grounded in Maren's specific psychology rather than being decorative. Rook's appearance is handled with the sideways quality the plan specifies: "The south path's been interesting lately," dropped into a gap in conversation and not elaborated upon. His standing in the dark field afterward is well-placed — observed through Maren's window, filtered through her attention, filed without resolution. This is exactly the right register for his character. The chapter's ending — Maren noticing her shoulders are not up around her ears, checking it "like she checked everything: is this real, is this reliable, what does it cost" — is quietly excellent. It honors the plan's instruction that Maren's arc is not "closed → open" but rather a gradual, involuntary noticing. The final image of sleeping through until morning is earned rather than announced. Where the chapter is slightly less successful: the middle section introducing the secondary characters (Bernard, Mae and Desmond, Fitz) is efficient but slightly list-like. These characters are introduced in a way that feels more like inventory than discovery, and while the details are specific, they don't quite achieve the same texture as Gil/Petra or Soo. This is a minor structural issue — the chapter has to introduce a lot of people and does so competently if not brilliantly. The dialogue is minimal but effective. Rook's single line is perfectly calibrated. The absence of dialogue in most of the chapter is a deliberate choice that works — this is a chapter about observation and accumulation, and Maren is watching more than talking. There are no instances of forced metaphor or purple prose. The writing is consistently grounded in the concrete and specific. The few moments of more lyrical observation ("a warmth that had an edge on it like it might cut if she held it wrong") are earned by their context and don't overstay their welcome. The chapter follows its plan faithfully: the settlement's texture is established, the secondary characters are introduced with specificity, Jonah's ease is shown, Bette's salt flaw is deployed, Rook's sideways observation appears, and the chapter ends with Maren's involuntary relaxation. The secondary undercurrent — what the settlement costs its people, what they don't say — is present throughout without being underlined. The cozy-horror balance is maintained: the warmth is real, the wrongness at the edges is real, and neither cancels the other. This is the story's central formal argument, and Chapter 4 makes it without announcing it. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 5 — *Render* The hunt went out at first light, four of them: Rook, who knew the ground; a woman named Clara who Maren hadn't properly met yet and who moved through the scrubland with a quiet efficiency that suggested she'd been doing this longer than most; a young man called Dav who was nervous in a way he was trying not to show and who kept his distance from Maren in the particular way people kept their distance from someone they'd been told was capable; and Maren, who had been told the night before by Bette, without asking, that she was going. She hadn't argued. She needed to see how it worked. The valley's scrubland extended north and east — south, Rook had explained over the previous evening's dinner, was where they didn't hunt, without elaborating on why, and she hadn't pushed. They went northeast along a path that was maintained enough to be deliberate and wild enough to look accidental. Rook moved at the front and pointed things out as they went — not hunting things, or not only hunting things. The particular moss on this stone face indicated the water table was higher here than it looked. The birds in the eastern copse had gone quiet in the last ten minutes, which could mean weather coming or could mean something moving through from the other side. She listened. She watched him read the land the way she'd watched experienced cooks read a service — the constant peripheral intake, the adjustments made before the problem fully announced itself. They found one at the edge of a ruined housing development, standing in what had been a back garden with its orientation toward something she couldn't determine. It was the plant-like kind, the slower kind, more fungal mass than the human architecture underneath — she could see the shape of an arm inside the growth if she looked and she chose not to look. Rook assessed it briefly and looked at Maren and she understood she was being offered the kill, which was either an initiation or a practicality and probably both. She went in from the side, away from the primary growth, and was quick about it. She named it first, silently, the way she always did. *Sandra,* she thought. *Okay, Sandra.* The kill was clean. Clara was already moving to assess the yield before the thing had finished falling, which was professional in a way Maren recognized and respected. On the way back Rook walked beside her and told her about a family of long-tailed tits he'd been tracking in the eastern copse since October. She listened. She found she was glad to listen to something that had nothing to do with anything. --- Back at the farmhouse Bette had the kitchen ready in the way that Bette always had things ready — not anticipatory, exactly, more as if the world had simply organised itself around her requirements. She looked at what they'd brought back and made several small sounds of assessment, not approval exactly, more the sounds of someone cross-referencing against an internal list, and then she and Maren began. Spore-meat processed differently than animal meat. Maren had known this from the petrol station but knowing it once in the dark under duress was different from working with it properly in a real kitchen in good light with someone who'd been doing it for eight months. The fungal structure separated from the underlying tissue more cleanly than she'd expected — Bette had a technique, a particular angle and pressure, that she explained while demonstrating with the matter-of-factness of someone teaching a practical skill that happened to be this skill. The meat itself was darker than animal meat and denser, with a fat distribution that behaved differently over heat — rendered slower, held its moisture longer. You couldn't rush it. It would tell you when it was ready and you had to wait. Maren worked and listened and let her hands find the logic of it. Jonah came in mid-morning and Bette handed him a task without ceremony, the way she handed everyone tasks, and he took it up and the three of them worked together in the kitchen with the radio silence of people who were busy. At some point Bette began narrating the recipe to herself, or to the room, the way she always did — *now then, you want the fat at a shimmer not a roll, it toughens if you push it* — and Maren listened to this and thought about the twenty-two years of students who had stood in kitchens and listened to exactly this voice saying something like this, and felt something she couldn't place, complicated and not unpleasant. She was about to ask the question she'd been carrying — not *how do you live with it,* which was too large, but something smaller: *did it take long, the first time, to stop tasting it differently* — when Jonah looked up from the carrots he was cutting and said: "Someone has moved the rabbit." Bette and Maren both looked at him. "The ceramic rabbit," he said. "On the windowsill. It was third from the left yesterday. It's fourth from the left today." Maren looked at the windowsill. She looked at the rabbit, which was a small brown ceramic thing with an expression of mild concern, currently in fourth position. She thought about Soo's two-millimeter correction when she'd moved one of them. "Maybe Soo moved it," she said. "Why would Soo move it?" "I don't know." "Soo doesn't move them," Jonah said, with the certainty of someone who'd been paying attention. "The arrangement is fixed. That's the whole point." He paused, carrot suspended. "Unless someone else moved it and Soo hasn't noticed yet, which means in about—" he considered the morning's timeline — "forty minutes there's going to be an incident." Maren looked at the rabbit. She looked at Jonah's face, which was completely serious. She looked at Bette, who had put down her reading glasses and was pressing her lips together in the way that preceded — She laughed. It came out before she'd decided anything about it, a real laugh, unguarded, the kind that embarrassed her slightly by its own volume. And then she was looking at what was on the butcher block in front of her. Both things were true at the same time. She was laughing and she was looking at what was on the butcher block and neither one canceled the other and she didn't know what to do with that except to keep going, so she kept going — she picked up her knife and she kept going. "Tell me when the incident happens," she said, and her voice came out steady, and she kept chopping. Bette watched her for a moment with the reading glasses held still in one hand. Then she put them down and went back to the stove. "Now then," she said. "You want to ask me something." Maren kept her eyes on the board. "Did it take long. To stop tasting it differently." Bette was quiet for a moment, the kind of quiet that meant she was being precise rather than hesitant. "I still taste it differently," she said. Maren's knife stopped. "I don't think that's wrong," Bette said. "I think—" she paused, and Maren heard her pick up the reading glasses again, put them down. "I think if it tasted the same I'd have lost something I'm not sure I should lose." She stirred something, slow and even. "What fades is the other thing. The thing underneath the taste." "What's the thing underneath." "What you're afraid it means about you." She said it simply, the way she said everything — not as wisdom, as her own specific experience, offered without expectation. "That fades. Takes a while. Didn't for me until about month four." A pause. "The food stays good. That part stays, too." Maren started chopping again. At the end of the morning Bette took out her recipe notebook — floral cover, slightly too cheerful — and wrote out what they'd made in her small careful cursive, cross-referencing quantities and timing with the focus of someone who believed this information had a future in which it would be useful. She wrote for a few minutes and then looked up. "What would you call it," she said. "If you were naming it." Maren thought about the dark broth and the slow fat and the way it had filled the kitchen with something you'd follow across a valley. "Something plain," she said. "Something that doesn't make a thing of itself." Bette nodded and wrote it down. They ate what they'd made for lunch, the three of them at the kitchen table. The food was good. It was very good. Maren ate her portion and tasted it, properly, with attention, and tasted it differently than she'd have tasted other things and tasted it as good, and both things were true, and she sat with that. Across the table Jonah was eating slowly, the way he always ate. He looked up and caught her looking. "The incident happened," he said. "While you were outside getting the herbs." "Was it bad?" "Soo was very calm about it," he said. "Which was somehow worse." Maren looked at her bowl. She picked up her spoon. Outside the kitchen window the afternoon light was doing something almost kind to the valley and the ceramic rabbit was back in its correct position on the sill, third from the left, its expression of mild concern restored to its proper place in the order of things.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkably accomplished piece of writing that largely delivers on the ambitious plan laid out in the story's intention document. Let me work through its qualities and weaknesses systematically. **Craft and Voice** The prose voice is consistently Maren's — dry, precise, observant, with the economy of someone who has learned not to waste motion. The narration trusts the reader throughout. When Maren names the mutant "Sandra" and thinks "Okay, Sandra," this is handled with exactly the lightness the plan calls for — present, not belabored, not explained. The line "She listened. She found she was glad to listen to something that had nothing to do with anything" is quietly excellent: it tells us about Maren's state without announcing it. The chapter's central formal achievement is the laugh-interrupted-by-the-butcher-block sequence. This is the plan's stated goal — "cozy and horror in the same breath" — and it lands. The transition is abrupt in exactly the right way: "She was laughing and she was looking at what was on the butcher block and neither one canceled the other." The prose doesn't editorialize. It describes and moves on. This is the story's central argument made in miniature, and it works. **Character Consistency** Maren is entirely consistent with her profile. Her naming of Sandra is handled with the right touch of self-awareness without self-pity. Her deflection — "Tell me when the incident happens" — is exactly the kind of redirection the profile describes: she makes herself useful rather than sitting with the feeling. Her question to Bette ("did it take long, the first time, to stop tasting it differently") is the smaller, more specific version of the larger question she can't ask, exactly as planned. Bette is excellent here. Her answer to Maren's question is the chapter's emotional peak, and it's handled with precision: "I still taste it differently. I don't think that's wrong." The follow-up — "What fades is the other thing. The thing underneath the taste" — is honest and particular rather than universally wise. The profile notes that Bette's answer should be "not comforting" and "particular to her and not universal," and this is achieved. The reading glasses business is used well as physical punctuation without becoming a tic. Jonah's ceramic rabbit observation is pitch-perfect for his character — the guileless directness, the careful attention to patterns, the complete seriousness about something that is objectively funny. His final line about Soo being "very calm about it, which was somehow worse" is genuinely funny and in his voice. **The Dialogue** The dialogue is strong throughout. It does not carry thematic weight efficiently — it carries it messily, sideways, the way the plan intends. Bette's exchange with Maren is the best dialogue in the chapter: it earns its weight because it arrives after the laugh, after the butcher block moment, and because Bette's answer is specific and not reassuring. The ceramic rabbit exchange is funny without winking. Jonah's final exchange is light and lands correctly. One minor weakness: "Now then. You want to ask me something" is slightly too convenient — Bette reading Maren's mind at exactly the right moment. It's not egregious, but it's the one moment where the scene feels slightly arranged rather than discovered. **The Plan's Requirements** The chapter follows its plan closely. The hunt is described efficiently. The central scene of Maren and Bette working together is the chapter's long center. The ethics arrive "improperly" — mid-laugh, as specified. Jonah appears to help. The laugh is interrupted by the butcher block. Maren keeps chopping. All of this is executed. The plan calls for Bette's answer to "not be wisdom, exactly — it's just what she thinks, and what she thinks is particular to her and not universal, and she doesn't offer it as though it applies to Maren. This is what makes it land." This is achieved almost exactly. **Weaknesses** The chapter is not without flaws. The opening paragraph is slightly over-constructed — the description of Dav keeping his distance "in the particular way people kept their distance from someone they'd been told was capable" is a bit too neat, a bit too self-consciously observed. It's the kind of sentence that calls attention to its own cleverness. The line "the world had simply organised itself around her requirements" for Bette is a mild purple moment — it's a slightly too-polished formulation for what is otherwise a grounded prose style. The ending, with the ceramic rabbit "restored to its proper place in the order of things," is the chapter's one moment of forced resonance. The phrase "order of things" reaches for significance in a way the rest of the chapter earns by not reaching. It's a small misstep in an otherwise well-controlled piece. The Rook section at the opening — the long-tailed tits — is good but brief. The plan calls for Rook's sideways observations to be an undercurrent, and here he's mostly just pleasant company. This is fine for this chapter, but his presence feels slightly underused. **Overall Assessment** This is a high-quality chapter that achieves its primary goals: the cozy and the horror coexist without canceling each other, the characters are consistent and specific, the central emotional moment (the laugh and the butcher block) is handled with real skill, and the dialogue earns its weight. The prose is controlled and the voice is consistent. Minor weaknesses — one too-neat sentence, one slightly forced ending image — don't significantly undermine the whole. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 6 — *Score* Rook found her after breakfast on the fourth day and said he was walking the south perimeter and wanted company. He said it the way he said most things — as a practical statement, information he was providing — and she found herself pulling on her jacket before she'd thought about whether she wanted to go. They went out through the side gate, the one that faced the field rather than the yard, and Rook latched it behind them with the habitual care of someone who had made the same motion enough times that it had become part of his body. The morning was cold and clear, the kind of cold that had weight to it, and the field was grey with frost that hadn't lifted yet from the shadow of the farmhouse. He didn't talk immediately, which she'd expected. He walked with the particular attention she'd come to recognize — reading the ground and the air and the movement of things — and she walked beside him and did the same and found it companionable in the way that shared work was companionable. She was not a person who needed conversation. Neither, it turned out, was Rook, though he came to it eventually, in pieces, in the way he did everything. "Long-tailed tits are back in the eastern copse," he said. "Whole family, I think. Same family I've been tracking." "From October." "From October. They go further south usually but this winter they've been staying close." He looked east briefly. "I don't know what to make of that." She waited. He was adding it to something, she could tell. She let him add. They reached the south fence, which was where the farmhouse garden ended and the maintained ground gave way to scrub and then, further out, the tree line. The fence was solid — repaired in several places with timber that was newer than the rest, work that had been done with care and in a hurry at different times. She ran her hand along one of the repairs and felt where the urgency had been. Rook stopped at a fence post and crouched down. He didn't explain what he was looking at immediately, and she crouched beside him and looked. Low on the post, and on the two posts she could see to either side, there were marks in the wood. Not claw marks. Not the kind of damage she was used to reading. A pattern of small pressures, evenly distributed, like something had stood close to the post for a long time and the contact had accumulated. "All along the south fence?" she said. "All along the south fence," he said. "Some on the east." She straightened up. The tree line was perhaps two hundred meters from where they stood. She looked at it, at the winter-thin trees and the dark between them. "When did you first notice the marks?" "Six weeks ago I noticed them. They might have been there before." He stood up and looked at the tree line with his slow, patient attention. "Six, seven weeks ago was also when they stopped attacking." She looked at him. "They used to come at the fence two, three times a month," he said. "We'd push them back. It was—" he seemed to consider the right word — "manageable. Predictable." He paused. "About six weeks ago it stopped. Nothing came at the fence." Another pause. "Instead they just—" "Stand," she said. "Stand." He nodded. "I've counted forty-three at the south line on one night. Just at the tree line, just facing in. They don't come further. They don't leave immediately." He looked at the marks on the fence post again. "Some nights I think the count is higher but I lose the number in the dark." She thought about the numbers. Eight months of eating them. Six weeks of changed behavior. Those numbers didn't fit together neatly and she turned them over looking for the way they fit and didn't find it. She filed this rather than said it. "Have you told the others?" she said. "I mention things," he said, in a tone that acknowledged this was not quite the same. "I don't want to—" he stopped. Started again. "They've built something here. It works. I don't want to be the thing that—" He left it there. She understood this. She didn't agree with it but she understood the architecture of it, the way you could know something and not say it because saying it would change the shape of what existed and the shape of what existed was worth protecting even if the protection was temporary. She had been doing this in various forms for two years. She was good at it and she was aware that good at it was not the same as right about it. "There's something else," Rook said. He was looking east now, not at the tree line. "I found a wood thrush last week. Sitting in the same tree six days running." She waited. "Facing south," he said. "Just sitting there, facing south. That's wrong — wrong direction for this time of year, and the sitting. They don't sit like that." He paused with the pause she'd come to understand meant he was listening for something that wasn't there. "I went back every morning. Six days." "What happened on the seventh day?" "It was gone." He looked at her. "I don't know why I'm telling you that." She thought she might know. She thought about the spore network, about what it was and how it worked and the things she'd read in the early months and the things people had said and the things that were conjecture and the things that weren't. She thought about forty-three bodies standing at a tree line facing inward with a patience that was not human patience. She thought about a wood thrush sitting in the wrong direction for six days. She didn't say any of it. She wasn't ready to say it, and she wasn't sure saying it would help, and she wasn't sure Rook didn't already know it and was managing it the same way she managed things, which was to keep adding to the column and not yet total it up. "Do the others know the count?" she said. "Forty-three." "No," he said. She nodded. They stood at the south fence for another minute and looked at the tree line, which gave nothing back — just the dark between the winter trees and the cold light above them and the absolute stillness of a thing that wasn't necessarily empty. Then Rook said: "I think we should head back. Bette'll have lunch on." --- Lunch was a root vegetable thing with field meat, slow-cooked since early morning, and it had filled the whole farmhouse with a smell that hit her in the face when they came through the door and did something uncomplicated to her chest. She stood in the doorway for a moment and just breathed it and Rook came in behind her and said, quietly, as if continuing a different conversation: "She does this thing with the fat — renders it twice. Takes longer but the flavor is—" "Different," Maren said. "Better," Rook said. And went to wash his hands. Dinner was the same pot, further reduced, with bread that Jonah and Fitz had apparently made in the afternoon with varying success — Jonah's half of the loaf was structurally sound and Fitz's half had a quality that Fitz described as rustic and that Bette described, with perfect pleasantness, as *a good first attempt.* This produced a debate about the nature of bread that occupied most of the table for twenty minutes and got loud enough that Mae put her hands over her ears in mock distress and Desmond pretended to take notes. Bernard laughed until he coughed. Maren sat in the middle of it and ate. She ate the stew and the bread, both halves, Jonah's and Fitz's — the rustic half last because it was genuinely terrible and she found she wanted to finish on it anyway — and she was warm and the table was full and someone had lit the candles down the center even though it wasn't dark yet because Gil said it was nearly dark enough and Petra said it wasn't but didn't blow them out. She looked at the window. The curtain didn't quite close on the left side — a gap of maybe six inches where the fabric fell short of the frame. Through it the field was grey-blue in the dusk and the tree line beyond the field was darker than the sky above it and she looked at it the way she'd learned to look at things that mattered, without looking directly. There was nothing there. She looked at the nothing and ate her bread and the candles threw orange light across the table and around her the conversation went on and she looked at the gap in the curtain and the dark field and the tree line. Then: a light. Faint and cold and blue-white, at the tree line. There and then not there — two seconds, maybe three, the particular luminescence she knew, and then the dark was just dark. She picked up her spoon. She didn't say anything. She ate the last of her stew and the candles burned and the conversation moved from bread to the storage room, inevitably, and Gil and Petra took up their positions with the enthusiasm of people grateful for a familiar argument, and Maren set down her spoon and looked at the window again. The dark was just dark. She reached for the salt.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 6 ("Score") is a remarkably controlled piece of writing that largely succeeds at the story's central formal ambition: holding cozy and horror simultaneously without letting either cancel the other. The chapter earns its effects through restraint and specificity rather than announcement. The opening perimeter walk is handled with considerable skill. Rook's characterization is faithful to his profile — he delivers information sideways, embedded in bird observations, and the wood thrush detail lands with genuine unease precisely because it's offered in the same register as the long-tailed tits. The marks on the fence posts ("a pattern of small pressures, evenly distributed, like something had stood close to the post for a long time and the contact had accumulated") is an excellent piece of horror writing — specific, physical, and deeply wrong without being melodramatic. Maren's internal processing of the numbers — "Eight months of eating them. Six weeks of changed behavior. Those numbers didn't fit together neatly and she turned them over looking for the way they fit and didn't find it. She filed this rather than said it" — is exactly the voice promised in the plan: dry, precise, someone who notices things and names them accurately. The decision not to say what she's thinking, and the narration's acknowledgment that this is a habit she knows is not the same as being right about it, is psychologically honest and avoids the trap of making her silence seem heroic. The transition from the perimeter walk to lunch is handled with a light touch. Rook's comment about the fat rendering is a small, perfect moment — it continues the conversation they were having without continuing it, and Maren's "Different" / Rook's "Better" exchange is economical and characterful. The dinner scene is where the chapter's formal argument is most fully realized. The bread debate, the candles, the argument about the storage room — these are specific and warm and genuinely funny in the way the plan describes (accidentally, at the characters' expense). Fitz's "rustic" bread and Bette's "good first attempt" is a small comic beat that lands cleanly. The detail of Maren eating both halves of the bread, finishing on the terrible half because she "found she wanted to finish on it anyway," is the kind of specific, slightly irrational human behavior that makes characters feel real. The bioluminescence at the tree line — "there and then not there — two seconds, maybe three" — is the chapter's horror payload, and it's delivered with exactly the right restraint. She sees it, she picks up her spoon, she doesn't say anything. The ending ("She reached for the salt") is quietly devastating: the salt is Bette's undersalting, which means Maren has been here long enough to have internalized the settlement's compensatory habits, and she does it without thinking, which is the story's real subject — the way she's already staying without deciding to. This is the chapter's best moment and it earns its weight. Some minor criticisms: The paragraph where Maren thinks about "the spore network, about what it was and how it worked and the things she'd read in the early months" is slightly too explicit — it gestures at a theory the story is wisely not stating, but the gesture itself is a little clunky, a moment where the narration is doing work that could be left to the reader. The line "the absolute stillness of a thing that wasn't necessarily empty" is the chapter's closest approach to purple prose — it's not quite wrong, but it's slightly more writerly than the surrounding prose and draws attention to itself. Rook's dialogue is consistently faithful to his profile. His "I mention things" in response to whether he's told the others is a perfect line — it acknowledges the gap between observation and communication without editorializing. The wood thrush detail is used well, though the "I don't know why I'm telling you that" / "She thought she might know" exchange is the chapter's one moment of slight over-signaling — the reader already knows why he's telling her, and the narration's acknowledgment of this is a small redundancy. The chapter follows its plan closely and faithfully. The plan called for the wrongness to become specific, for the horror to earn its weight, and for the warmth of dinner to coexist fully with the walk — all of this is achieved. The plan's note that "the warmth of dinner is not diminished by the walk — they coexist fully, which is the story's central formal argument" is realized in the structure of the chapter itself. Characters are consistent with their profiles throughout. Rook's sideways delivery, his patience, his discomfort with urgency — all present. Maren's filing rather than saying, her dry internal voice, her noticing — all present. The settlement's accumulated specificity (the storage room argument, the candles, the bread debate) feels earned rather than decorative. The chapter is not quite a masterpiece — the slight over-signaling in the Rook exchange and the one clunky paragraph about the spore network hold it back — but it is very good work, executing a difficult tonal balance with consistent skill. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 7 — *Hold* She was awake when Rook's hand came down on her shoulder. She'd been awake for an hour, maybe more, lying in the not-quite-dark of the room and looking at the ceiling and doing the inventory in a way that had stopped being about her body and had become about the house — every sound, its source, its distance, whether it had happened before at this time and in this way. The house had its own rhythms by now and she knew most of them. The thing she'd been waiting for was the sound that didn't fit. She hadn't heard anything. She'd just been awake. "South fence," Rook said. One hand on her shoulder, her name said once. He was already moving back toward the door. She was up and dressed and outside in less than two minutes, which was not a record but was close. The frost had come down hard overnight and the field was white and flat and it threw back enough of the sky's ambient light that she could see clearly, more clearly than she'd expected, as if the field had been arranged for visibility. Rook was at the south fence already. She crossed the field and came to stand beside him. She had prepared herself, on the way across the field, for a number. Forty-three, which was Rook's highest count. Perhaps more — perhaps significantly more. She had prepared herself for that. She had not prepared herself for what the number looked like as a thing in the world. They were at the tree line, which was two hundred meters from where she stood, and they extended along it in both directions as far as she could see in the white-dark. Her count lost itself immediately — not forty-three, not fifty, a number that stopped being useful as a number and became something more like a condition. The bioluminescence came from all of them at once, low and cold and blue, so that the tree line itself appeared to be lit from within, a long cold burn stretching east and west along the darkness. They were oriented toward the farmhouse. They were perfectly still. Rook said nothing. She said nothing. They stood at the fence and looked at the thing at the tree line for long enough that her feet began to ache from the cold coming up through the ground. "They're not at the fence," she said. "No," Rook said. "They're not moving." "No." She breathed carefully. The cold air came into her lungs and she noted its quality — dry, clean, no wind. In the stillness the tree line was completely silent. Whatever number of bodies stood at it, they made no sound at all. No movement, no displaced branches, nothing. Just the cold blue light. "Wake the others," she said. "Yes," he said. He didn't move immediately.
